description: Learn how to make a table with visual interestingness.
text: In this lesson, we've explored the power of tables for presenting sports data using the gt package in R. We started by learning how to create basic tables from our dataset, then progressively enhanced them with various features. We covered important techniques such as adding meaningful headers and labels, styling text elements for better readability, and incorporating source information. We learned how to use tab_style() to customize specific parts of our table, including making headers bold and adding bottom borders. We also explored how to improve the table's overall appearance by using row striping and removing unnecessary lines. Importantly, we practiced formatting numerical data appropriately, converting raw numbers into percentages. Finally, we learned how to use color strategically to highlight specific data points, enhancing the table's ability to tell a story. Throughout the process, we saw how tables can effectively present detailed information while still guiding the viewer's attention to key points. Remember, while tables may seem simple, thoughtful design and formatting can make them powerful tools for communicating sports analytics insights.

description: Learn how to make a table with visual interestingness.
text: - `gt`: An R package used for creating customizable tables in data analysis and presentation. - `tab_header()`: A function in gt used to add a title and subtitle to a table. - `cols_label()`: A function in gt used to rename column headers in a table. - `tab_style()`: A function in gt used to apply custom styles to specific parts of a table. - `tab_source_note()`: A function in gt used to add source information or notes at the bottom of a table.

description: Learn how to make a lot of graphics with little code.
text: The goal of this lesson is to introduce you to faceting, a powerful technique in ggplot2 for creating multiple related charts in a single visualization. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand how to use facet_wrap() and facet_grid() to create small multiples, allowing you to compare trends across different categories or groups. You'll learn when to use faceting, how to implement it with various chart types, and how to customize faceted plots for better readability. We'll apply these concepts to real college football data, analyzing offensive performance across Big Ten teams. This lesson will enhance your ability to present complex, multi-dimensional data in a clear and intuitive format, enabling viewers to easily compare patterns and trends across different subsets of your data.

description: Learn how to make a lot of graphics with little code.
text: Sometimes the easiest way to spot a trend is to chart a bunch of small things side by side. Edward Tufte, one of the most well known data visualization thinkers on the planet, calls this "small multiples" where ggplot calls this a facet wrap or a facet grid, depending. Who had the best offense in the Big Ten last season? We could answer this a number of ways, but the best way to show people would be visually. Let's use Small Multiples. As always, we start with libraries. We're going to use the logs of college football games last season. Let's narrow our pile and look just at the Big Ten. The first thing we can do is look at a line chart, like we have done in previous lessons.
code:
logs <- read_csv("https://mattwaite.github.io/sportsdatafiles/footballlogs25.csv")
big10 <- logs |> filter(conference == "Big Ten Conference")

description: Learn how to make a lot of graphics with little code.
text: We'll do something simple like a line chart of `avg` -- the yards per play on offense -- and we're going to group on `team`. And, not surprisingly, we get a hairball. Who is the best offense week in and out in the Big Ten? We could color certain lines, but that would limit us to focus on one team. **What if we did all of them at once?** We do that with a `facet_wrap`. Will that give us an answer?
code:
ggplot() + 
  geom_____(data=____, aes(x=date, y=____, group=____))

description: Learn how to make a lot of graphics with little code.
text: The only thing we MUST pass into a `facet_wrap` is what thing we're going to separate them out by. In this case, we precede that field with a tilde, so in our case we want the Team field. Answer: Not immediately clear, but we can look at this and analyze it. We could add a piece of annotation to help us out.
code:
ggplot() + 
  geom_____(data=____, aes(x=date, y=____, group=____)) + 
  facet_wrap(~____)

description: Learn how to make a lot of graphics with little code.
text: Let's add a line to every chart that's the conference average shooting percentage. Copy that number, and we're going to add it into the facet chart with a `geom_hline`. What do you see here? How do teams compare? How do they change over time? I'm not asking you these questions because they're an assignment -- I'm asking because that's exactly what this chart helps answer. Your brain will immediately start making those connections.
code:
big10 |> summarise(mean(avg))
ggplot() + 
  geom_hline(yintercept=____, color="blue") + 
  geom_____(data=____, aes(x=date, y=____, group=____)) + 
  facet_wrap(~____)

description: Learn how to make a lot of graphics with little code.
text: Facet grids allow us to put teams on the same plane, versus just repeating them. And we can specify that plane as vertical or horizontal. For example, here's our chart from above, but using facet_grid to stack them.

description: Learn how to make a lot of graphics with little code.
text: And here they are next to each other: Two notes: 1. We'd have some work to do with the labeling on this -- we'll get to that -- but you can see where this is valuable comparing a group of things. One warning: Don't go too crazy with this or it loses it's visual power. 2. There's nothing special about a line chart with faceting. You can facet any kind of ggplot chart.
code:
ggplot() + 
  geom_hline(yintercept=____, color="blue") + 
  geom_____(data=____, aes(x=date, y=____, group=____)) + 
  facet_grid(team ~ .)
ggplot() + 
  geom_hline(yintercept=____, color="blue") + 
  geom_____(data=____, aes(x=date, y=____, group=____)) + 
  facet_grid(. ~ team)

description: Learn how to make a lot of graphics with little code.
text: In this lesson, we've explored the power of faceting in ggplot2 to create small multiples, a technique that allows for easy comparison across multiple categories or groups. We started by creating a basic line chart of offensive performance for Big Ten football teams, then transformed it into a faceted plot using facet_wrap(). We learned how to add context to our faceted plots by including a reference line for the conference average. We then explored the difference between facet_wrap() and facet_grid(), understanding how each can be used to arrange our small multiples either in a grid or in rows/columns. We saw how faceting can reveal patterns and trends that might be obscured in a single, overlapping chart. Throughout the lesson, we applied these techniques to real college football data, demonstrating how faceting can provide insights into team performance over time. Remember, while faceting is a powerful tool, it's important to use it judiciously to maintain clarity in your visualizations. As we progressed, we also touched on the importance of proper labeling and the versatility of faceting across different chart types.

description: Learn how to make a lot of graphics with little code.
text: - Faceting: A technique in ggplot2 for creating multiple related charts in a single visualization, allowing for easy comparison across categories or groups. - Small Multiples: A series of similar graphs or charts using the same scale and axes, allowing for comparison across different categories or time periods. - `facet_wrap()`: A ggplot2 function that wraps a series of plots into a rectangular layout. - `facet_grid()`: A ggplot2 function that creates a grid of plots with fixed horizontal and vertical axes. - Tilde (~): In the context of faceting, used to specify the variable(s) by which to facet the plot.

description: Learn how to make multiple charts into one graphic.
text: The goal of this lesson is to introduce you to the patchwork package in R, a powerful tool for combining multiple ggplot2 charts into a single, cohesive visualization. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand how to create individual plots, save them as objects, and arrange them together using patchwork's intuitive syntax. You'll learn how to stack plots vertically or place them side by side, add overall titles and annotations, and apply consistent theming across combined plots. We'll apply these concepts to real college football data, creating a comparative visualization of rushing and passing performance across Big Ten teams. This lesson will enhance your ability to create complex, multi-faceted visualizations that tell a more comprehensive story with your sports data, allowing you to present related information in a clear, unified format.

description: Learn how to make multiple charts into one graphic.
text: Sometimes you have two or three (or more) charts that by themselves aren't very exciting and are really just one chart that you need to merge together. It would be nice to be able to arrange them programmatically and not have to mess with it in Adobe Illustrator. Good news. There is. It's called `patchwork`, and it's pretty easy to use. You installed patchwork way back in the beginning, but if you're having problems just go to the console in R Studio and run `install.packages("patchwork")`. We'll use the college football game data we've used before. Load it. For this, we're going to total up the rushing yards and attempts for each game, as well as the passing yards and attempts, to get a top 5 list of the best rushing and passing offenses in the Big Ten last season.
code:
games <- read_csv("https://mattwaite.github.io/sportsdatafiles/footballlogs25.csv")
totals <- games |> 
  filter(conference == "Big Ten Conference") |> 
  group_by(team) |> 
  summarize(
    total_rushes = sum(rushing_att),
    total_rush_yds = sum(rushing_yds),
    total_passes = sum(passing_att),
    total_pass_yds = sum(passing_yds)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    rushing_avg = total_rush_yds/total_rushes,
    passing_avg = total_pass_yds/total_passes
  )

description: Learn how to make multiple charts into one graphic.
section: Exercise 1: Making bar charts into objects
text: Okay, now to do this I need to **save my plots to an object**. We do this the same way we save things to a dataframe -- with the arrow aka the assignment operator. We'll make two identical bar charts, both using the `totals` dataframe. One with be the best passing team by average and one will be the best rushing by average. We'll call them bar1 and bar2.
code:
____ <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=____, aes(x=reorder(team, passing_avg), weight=passing_avg)) + 
  coord_flip()

____ <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=____, aes(x=reorder(team, rushing_avg), weight=rushing_avg)) + 
  coord_flip()

description: Learn how to make multiple charts into one graphic.
section: Exercise 2: Making two charts into one plot
text: With patchwork, we can use some basic symbols to lay out plots. Because we have two plots, all we need to do is use a `+` to add our two plots together. Remember what you named them above?

description: Learn how to make multiple charts into one graphic.
section: Exercise 3: Making two charts into one plot, but stacked
text: We can also stack them on top of each other with /.

description: Learn how to make multiple charts into one graphic.
text: To make these publishable, we should add headlines, chatter, decent labels, credit lines, etc. But to do this, we have to do a little thinking. It's easiest to think about what things go on individual charts and which things go on the whole plot. Things that go on individual charts: x and y axis labels and pre-made themes. Things that go on the whole plot: title, subtitle, caption and the theming necessary to make those look good. Let's just start with labels and a pre-set theme on each plot.
code:
bar1 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=totals, aes(x=reorder(team, passing_avg), weight=passing_avg)) +
  coord_flip() + 
  labs(
    x="", 
    y="Average passing yards"
    ) + 
  theme_minimal() 

bar2 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=totals, aes(x=reorder(team, rushing_avg), weight=rushing_avg), color="grey") +
  coord_flip() +  
  labs(
    x="", 
    y="Average rushing yards") + 
  theme_minimal()

bar1 + bar2

description: Learn how to make multiple charts into one graphic.
text: What's missing here? To publish this, we need a headline, chatter, source line and credit line (aka title, subtitle and caption). With patchwork, we have to add these to the plot itself -- *not* to the individual charts. You do that with `plot_annotation`, which works very similarly to labs that we've used all along. Now, we need to add some theming to those plot text elements. To do that, we use `&` and add theme. Don't worry much about what goes in the theme for now -- we'll cover that in finishing touches. For now, just know we're going to make the title/headline bold and bigger, the subtitle/chatter a little smaller. The important part right now is that you know this is possible and this is how you do it.
code:
bar1 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=totals, aes(x=reorder(team, passing_avg), weight=passing_avg)) +
  coord_flip() + 
  labs(
    x="", 
    y="Average passing yards"
    ) + 
  theme_minimal()

bar2 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=totals, aes(x=reorder(team, rushing_avg), weight=rushing_avg), color="grey") +
  coord_flip() +  
  labs(
    x="", 
    y="Average rushing yards") + 
  theme_minimal()

bar1 + bar2 + 
  _________(
    title = "This is a title and we need to tell a story with it",
    subtitle = "This is the subtitle and we need to add context to the chart here.",
    caption = "Source: Sports Reference | By Matt Waite"
  )
bar1 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=totals, aes(x=reorder(team, passing_avg), weight=passing_avg)) +
  coord_flip() + 
  labs(
    x="", 
    y="Average passing yards"
    ) + 
  theme_minimal() 

bar2 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=totals, aes(x=reorder(team, rushing_avg), weight=rushing_avg), color="grey") +
  coord_flip() +  
  labs(
    x="", 
    y="Average rushing yards") + 
  theme_minimal()

bar1 + bar2 + 
  plot_annotation(
    title = "This is a title and we need to tell a story with it",
    subtitle = "This is the subtitle and we need to add context to the chart here.",
    caption = "Source: Sports Reference | By Matt Waite"
  )  & 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

description: Learn how to make multiple charts into one graphic.
text: The last thing we're missing is color. Our eyes aren't drawn to anything (except maybe the top and bottom). So we need to help that. **A bar chart without context or color to draw attention to something isn't much of a bar chart**. Same with a line chart -- if your line chart has one line, no context, no color, it's going to fare poorly. To get some color and context in this, we're going to focus on ... a local team ... and add another geom_bar to our two bar charts using that data. We can then assign that one bar a color. No surprise, the data reflects what happened last season -- we could run it, we could not throw it.
code:
nu <- totals |> filter(team == "Nebraska")

bar1 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=totals, aes(x=reorder(team, passing_avg), weight=passing_avg), fill="grey") +
  geom_bar(data=nu, aes(x=reorder(team, passing_avg), weight=passing_avg), fill="red") + 
  coord_flip() + 
  labs(
    x="", 
    y="Average passing yards"
    ) + 
  theme_minimal() 

bar2 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=totals, aes(x=reorder(team, rushing_avg), weight=rushing_avg), fill="grey") +
  geom_bar(data=nu, aes(x=reorder(team, rushing_avg), weight=rushing_avg), fill="red") + 
  coord_flip() +  
  labs(
    x="", 
    y="Average rushing yards") + 
  theme_minimal()

bar1 + bar2  + 
  plot_annotation(
    title = "This is a title and we need to tell a story with it",
    subtitle = "This is the subtitle and we need to add context to the chart here.",
    caption = "Source: Sports Reference | By Matt Waite"
  ) & 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

description: Learn how to make multiple charts into one graphic.
text: In this lesson, we've explored the power of the patchwork package to create complex, multi-chart visualizations in R. We started by creating individual bar charts representing passing and rushing averages for Big Ten football teams, then learned how to combine these charts using patchwork's simple '+' and '/' operators. We progressed to adding overall titles, subtitles, and captions to our combined plots using plot_annotation(), and discovered how to apply consistent theming across the entire visualization. We also learned the importance of saving individual plots as objects for easier manipulation and combination. Throughout the lesson, we emphasized the need for context and visual hierarchy in our charts, introducing techniques like using color to highlight specific data points (in this case, Nebraska's performance). By the end, we created a comprehensive visualization that not only displayed data but told a story about team performance. Remember, while patchwork makes it easy to combine plots, the real skill lies in choosing which plots to combine and how to present them to best communicate your insights.

description: Learn how to make multiple charts into one graphic.
text: - patchwork: An R package that allows for easy combination of multiple ggplot2 charts into a single visualization. - plot_annotation(): A patchwork function used to add overall titles, subtitles, and captions to combined plots. - `+` operator in patchwork: Used to place plots side by side in a patchwork arrangement. - `/` operator in patchwork: Used to stack plots vertically in a patchwork arrangement. - `&` operator in patchwork: Used to apply theme elements to all plots in a patchwork arrangement.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 27: Shapes as storytelling
description: Learn how to draw attention to some points.
text: The goal of this lesson is to introduce you to the technique of encircling points on a scatterplot using the ggalt package in R and annotation with rectangles and `geom_rect`. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand how to use the `geom_encircle()` and `annotate` functions to draw attention to specific data points or groups of points in your visualizations. You'll learn how to create basic scatterplots, layer additional data points, and then highlight key information using circles and text labels. We'll apply these concepts to real college basketball data, analyzing player performance in terms of shooting percentage and rebounds. This lesson will enhance your ability to create more informative and visually compelling scatterplots, allowing you to guide your audience's attention to the most important aspects of your data story in sports analytics.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 27: Shapes as storytelling
description: Learn how to draw attention to some points.
text: One thing we've talked about all semester is drawing attention to the thing you want to draw attention to. We've used color and labels to do that so far. Let's add another layer to it -- a shape around the point or points you want to highlight. Remember: The point of all of this is to draw the eye to what you are trying to show your reader. You want people to see the story you are trying to tell. It's not hard to draw a shape in ggplot -- it is a challenge to put it in the right place. But, there is a library to the rescue that makes this super easy -- `ggalt`. Like usual, you already installed this if you followed the install instructions at the beginning of the course. But if you're having trouble getting this to work, go to your console in R Studio and install it with `devtools::install_github("mattwaite/ggalt")` There's a bunch of things that `ggalt` does, but one of the most useful for us is the function `encircle`. Let's dive in. Let's say we want to highlight Brice Williams and his place in college basketball last season. Let's take a look at last season's player data. And while we're loading it, let's filter out anyone who hasn't played 100 minutes. Let's make a standard scatterplot of points and player efficiency rating.
code:
players <- read_csv("https://mattwaite.github.io/sportsdatafiles/players25.csv") |>
  filter(MP > 100)

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 27: Shapes as storytelling
description: Learn how to draw attention to some points.
text: Who is hitting shots, and who is using his chances to score efficiently is the question we're after here. Our data is players, and our two fields are PTS and PER. We can see right away that there are some dots at the edges -- lots of points but maybe they aren't contributing elsewhere. Where are the Huskers in this? We can get that with filtering and layering.
code: ggplot() + geom_point(data=____, aes(x=____, y=____))

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 27: Shapes as storytelling
description: Learn how to draw attention to some points.
section: Exercise 2: Adding the Huskers as a layer
text: Like we have done in the past, let's make a dataframe of Nebraska players and add a layer to our graph that puts Nebraska's dots in red. While we're at it, we'll make everyone else grey. And let's label the Nebraska players. Given that this chart is about Brice Williams, I'll give you three guesses who that dot is scoring a lot of points really efficiently. Your first two guesses don't count and his name rhymes with rice illiams. But just this quickly, we're on the path to something publishable. We'll need to label a dot and we'll need to drop the default grey and add some headlines and all that. And, for the most part, we've got a solid chart. But what if we could really draw the eye to a dot or group of them? In `ggalt`, there is a geom called `geom_encircle`, which ... does what you think it does. It encircles all the dots in a dataset.
code:
nu <- players |> filter(Team == "Nebraska Cornhuskers Men's")

ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=____, aes(x=____, y=____), color="grey") +
  geom_point(data=____, aes(x=____, y=____), color="red") +
  geom_text_repel(data=____, aes(x=PTS, y=PER, label=Player))

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 27: Shapes as storytelling
description: Learn how to draw attention to some points.
text: Now we'll add geom_encircle and we'll just copy the data and the aes from our nu geom_point. Then, we need to give the encirclement a shape using s_shape -- which is a number between 0 and 1 -- and then how far away from the dots to draw the circle using expand, which is another number between 0 and 1. Let's start with `s_shape` .03 and `expand` .03. Getting to the right number involves a lot of trial and error messing with that number. Sometimes you'll want a bigger circle -- closer to one -- and sometimes you want small. That's not bad. If you disagree, now you begin the process of fiddling with `s_shape` and `expand` until you get it just right.
code:
bw <- nu |> filter(Player == "Brice Williams")

ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=____, aes(x=____, y=____), color="grey") +
  geom_point(data=____, aes(x=____, y=____), color="red") +
  geom_text_repel(data=nu, aes(x=PTS, y=PER, label=Player)) + 
  geom_encircle(data=bw, aes(x=____, y=____), s_shape=.03, expand=.03, colour="red")

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 27: Shapes as storytelling
description: Learn how to draw attention to some points.
text: What if, instead of a circle, you wanted to add a rectangle to highlight the zone of good players (or something like that). Rectangles work great to highlight zones or, if you have time as your x axis, events and time periods. Think of it this way -- if you had a line chart of Nebraska's wins over time, you could add rectangles over the years for certain coaches you wanted to highlight.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 27: Shapes as storytelling
description: Learn how to draw attention to some points.
section: Exercise 4: Using a rectangle instead of a circle
text: So how do you make rectangles? With annotate and some interpolation. What's interpolation? It's looking at the x and y axis and interpreting where to draw the lines. Looking at our previous work, what if we use 200 points and a 20 PER rating as the floor for our good zone. The end ceiling is the top of the chart -- which ggplot can conveniently just use infinity for that. One more thing? We should label our zone. It looks like there's a spot at 300 points and 35 PER to fit some text. Now let's clean this up and make it presentable with labels, a theme and some alterations to that theme.
code:
ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=players, aes(x=PTS, y=PER), color="grey") +
  geom_point(data=nu, aes(x=PTS, y=PER), color="red") +
  geom_text_repel(data=nu, aes(x=PTS, y=PER, label=Player)) +
  annotate("rect", fill = "blue", alpha = 0.1, 
        xmin = ____, xmax = Inf,
        ymin = ____, ymax = Inf) +
  geom_text(aes(x=____, y=____, label="Good player zone"), size=3)
ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=players, aes(x=PTS, y=PER), color="grey") +
  geom_point(data=nu, aes(x=PTS, y=PER), color="red") +
  geom_text_repel(data=nu, aes(x=PTS, y=PER, label=Player)) +
  annotate("rect", fill = "blue", alpha = 0.1, 
        xmin = ____, xmax = Inf,
        ymin = ____, ymax = Inf) +
  geom_text(aes(x=300, y=35, label="Good player zone"), size=3) +
  labs(title="Brice Williams was Nebraska's MVP last season", subtitle="Not just Nebraska's best, but one of the best in the Big Ten.", x="Points scored", y="Player Efficiency Rating") + 
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
    )

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 27: Shapes as storytelling
description: Learn how to draw attention to some points.
text: In this lesson, we've explored the powerful technique of encircling points on a scatterplot using the ggalt package in R and making rectangles with annotate. We started by creating a basic scatterplot of college basketball player performance, focusing on points and efficiency. We then learned how to layer additional data points to highlight specific teams or players. The key skill we developed was using geom_encircle() and annotate to draw attention to particular data points, in this case, showcasing Brice Williams exceptional performance for Nebraska. Throughout the lesson, we emphasized the importance of visual hierarchy and guiding the viewer's eye to the most crucial information. We also touched on how to make our visualizations more polished and publication-ready by adding appropriate titles, labels, and theming. Remember, the goal of these techniques is not just to present data, but to tell a compelling story with your visualizations.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 27: Shapes as storytelling
description: Learn how to draw attention to some points.
text: - `ggalt`: An extension package for ggplot2 that provides additional geoms and statistical transformations. - `geom_encircle()`: A function from ggalt that allows you to draw a circle or shape around specified data points on a plot. - `s_shape`: A parameter in geom_encircle() that controls the shape of the encircling line, with values between 0 and 1. - `expand`: A parameter in geom_encircle() that determines how far the encircling line extends from the data points.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 28: Make your own blog
description: Learn how to show what you can do on the internet for an audience.
text: The goal of this lesson is to introduce you to creating and publishing your own data analysis blog using Quarto and GitHub Pages. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand how to set up a Quarto blog project, create blog posts with R code and analysis, preview your site locally, and publish it online for free using GitHub. You'll learn the importance of blogging for aspiring sports analysts, how to structure your blog posts to showcase your work, and best practices for naming and organizing your content. This lesson will enhance your ability to share your sports data insights with a wider audience, building your portfolio and demonstrating your skills to potential employers or collaborators in the field of sports analytics.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 28: Make your own blog
description: Learn how to show what you can do on the internet for an audience.
text: If you listen to the [Measurables Podcast](https://www.measurablespod.com/podcast) for about two episodes, you'll detect a pattern. The host asks each guest how they got started in sports analytics. To a one, they'll say they found public data and started blogging about their analysis of it. For nearly every single guest, this is their path into the field. They started messing around with data in a toolset, found something interesting and wrote a post about what they found and how they found it. Other analysts noticed it, or a hiring manager liked what they read, and the rest is history, as they say. So, let's do that. Let's get you a blog so you can post your work. Here's our requirements: 1. This doesn't cost you anything. 2. There's zero maintenance work or upkeep. No servers to manage. No account to pay for. 3. Since you're going to be writing about your code, you should be able to create your blog posts in R Studio.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 28: Make your own blog
description: Learn how to show what you can do on the internet for an audience.
text: With those requirements in mind, we're going to use a library called Quarto, which supercharges R Markdown to make documents, websites, blogs and books. It's new, and growing, and you can do a lot with it. You'll first go to [Quarto's website](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/) and wait just one moment before you do anything. Before you install Quarto, you should close R Studio. So close R Studio, install it, restart R Studio and come back to this tutorial. After that, we're ready to start making a blog. To do that, go to File \> New Project and select New Directory (or Version Control if you know what you're doing). In the Project Type view, find "Quarto Blog". Mine will probably look different than yours, but below is what the line you're looking for looks like. Name the directory you're going to put this into. Keep it simple. Blog is fine. Then decide where on your computer you're going to put it. You should put this where you've been putting all your homework for this class. When you hit Create Project, you should get an R Studio screen. You might have two files open -- _quarto.yml and index.qmd. If you don't, open them (you can use the files pane in the bottom right). The important one for now is _quarto.yml. This file will look different depending on some choices you make. The choices you need to make: * The website title should be yours. * The Github account (more on this soon) and Twitter handles should be yours. * If you want a custom favicon logo, you'll have to include one. You can ignore this for a while. * You should pick a [not-default theme](https://quarto.org/docs/websites/website-blog.html#themes). If you want to have themes for light and dark mode, the code below handles that. If you want just one theme, the default code you get in your _quarto.yml file does that. * Add the execute: freeze bits below to your file. This way, your code only executes when you change it. This will become more important later when you have multiple posts. ``` project: type: website website: title: "Matt Waite's Collection of Miscellany" description: "A blog about random fiddling with code and data." navbar: right: - about.qmd - icon: github href: https://github.com/mattwaite - icon: twitter href: https://twitter.com/mattwaite favicon: logo.png format: html: theme: light: litera dark: cyborg css: styles.css editor: visual execute: freeze: auto ```

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 28: Make your own blog
description: Learn how to show what you can do on the internet for an audience.
text: Under the files tab on the lower right, you'll see a folder named posts. In there, you'll find two folders with sample posts in them. There's not much to them, but they're useful to look at before we delete them. To create a post, you're going to first create a folder for it. **The folder name matters because it's going to be part of your url.** For this to work your folder name has to do a couple of things: * Contain only lowercase letters and dashes instead of spaces. So My Awesome Post will not work. It should be my-awesome-post. * Contain words that are in your post, and would be something people would search for. If your post is about Nebraska women's run into the NCAA Tournament, make sure your post title and folder name contains those words. * In other words, **you need to think about what your post is about before you make this folder**. Can you change it later? Yes. But the sooner you start focusing on a topic the better. After you have created the folder, go to the button next to the create folder button and create a Quarto Document. Name that document index.qmd. **DO NOT NAME IT CAPITAL I INDEX. IT'S LOWER CASE I.** At the top, you will have something called a yaml header, which are things that set up the page. They are quite simple to figure out. Here is an example you should copy and edit for all of your posts. ``` --- title: "CHANGE THIS TO BE THE TITLE OF YOUR POST" author: "THIS IS YOUR NAME" date: '2022-XX-XX' categories: [lowercaseword, lowercaseword, lowercaseword] image: "image.png" format: html: code-fold: true --- ``` For us, the cold-fold bit is important, and it is not standard. This will allow users to see your code that makes your graphics, without displaying it. Users can decide if they want to see the code. Now that you have a folder and an index.qmd file with a proper yaml header, you're ready to write your post. This document works just the same as any R Markdown file we've worked with all semester. Create text between code blocks, and your code blocks will execute to produce graphics.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 28: Make your own blog
description: Learn how to show what you can do on the internet for an audience.
text: While you are creating your site, you're going to want to see it. Quarto will spin up a web server on your computer and render your site in a browser with a button. This will give you the chance to see what it will look like when it is finally published. For this step, you want to Preview your website.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 28: Make your own blog
description: Learn how to show what you can do on the internet for an audience.
text: Quarto static site generator. What that means is that Quarto is going to take your Markdown files and create static html. What does static html mean? It means there's no server creating it on the fly -- that's called dynamic html -- so this can be hosted on the simplest of servers. Simple means cheap or even free. Publishing takes a lot of steps to get set up, but once it is, it's easy. **Step 1: Get a GitHub account** Go to [GitHub](https://GitHub.com/) and sign up for an account. NOTE: Your username will be part of your website address, and you could potentially be sending this to employers. I'm sure blaze420rryday has been your handle since middle school, but do you want an employer to see that? My GitHub user name is mattwaite. See what I'm getting at here? **Step 2: Set up your website repository** For the low low price of nothing, GitHub will host a website for you, and we are going to take them up on that bargain. There's several tricks to getting this to work, but none of them are hard. They just require you to be specific. To start, you can click on the plus sign in the top right corner or hit the green new button, depending on what your page looks like vs mine (I'm reasonably active GitHub user, so mine will look different from yours). In the create a new repository page, **the name of your respository needs to follow this pattern**: yourusernamehere.github.io where yourusernamehere is ... your username. So my site is mattwaite.github.io because my username is mattwaite. This is why you do not want to select swaggylovedoctor as your username, no matter how attached to it you are. Your employment chances are zero with something dumb like that. After you've named the repository correctly, leave it public, check add .gitignore, and then select a gitignore template. Type R into the search, select it, and the create repository button will become active. Click create repository. **Step 3:** Don't close your GitHub window. In a new tab, [download the GitHub Desktop App](https://desktop.github.com/) and install it. GitHub is a version control system and a social network mixed together. Version control is like Track Changes in Word, but on steroids. If you plan on a career in analytics or data science, GitHub is a skill you will have to learn, but it's beyond the scope of this book. To work around this, we're going to use GitHub Desktop to manage our interactions with GitHub. Open GitHub Desktop and log into your GitHub account in the app. To get your repository into GitHub Desktop, go back to your tab with your GitHub repository in it. Find the Code button and click it. Click on the Open With GitHub Desktop button. In the window that pops up, we need to set the Local Path. **This path needs to be the same place your blog project is located.** In other words, there needs to be a folder where you have your Blog folder, and you're going to put it in the containing folder, **NOT IN YOUR BLOG FOLDER**. These two folders need to be side by side with each other in the same folder. Click the Choose button and navigate to the folder where your blog project is located. Then, click Clone. **Step 4:** Let's get your site on the internet. Switch back to your blog project in R Studio. We're going to output your site and upload it to GitHub. To do this, we need to change _quarto.yml. We need to tell your website project that you want to publish to your GitHub folder, not the default. Open _quarto.yml in your blog project. At the top, add this line, replacing yourusername with ... your user name. This says publish to your GitHub project that you put in the same folder as your blog project: ``` project: type: website output-dir: ../YOURUSERNAME.github.io ``` Save _quarto.yml. Once you are ready to build your site, you need to click on the Render Website button. If it worked, you should see output in the Background Jobs window, which is in the same window as the Console. **Step 5** Now go to the GitHub Desktop App. You should see something that looks like this, though yours will be different from mine. The important part is you see changes to commit. This is where you will commit files to your repository. GitHub is a two-step process for getting files from your computer to GitHub. The first is committing the files. To do that, add a message in the box next to your avatar (where it says Summary (required)). Then click Commit to main (mine says master because it's an older repository before GitHub switched to using main by default). The second step is to push it to GitHub. You do that on the screen that appears after you have committed your files. It should say you have No Local Changes, which is good because you sent them to GitHub. Now click Push origin (or, if this is the first time, it might say Publish Branch). In a few minutes, you should be able to go to username.github.io in a browser and you'll see your site. [Here's mine](https://mattwaite.github.io/).

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 28: Make your own blog
description: Learn how to show what you can do on the internet for an audience.
text: In this lesson, we've explored the process of creating and publishing your own sports analytics blog using Quarto and GitHub Pages. We started by discussing the importance of blogging in the sports analytics community and how it can help launch careers. We then walked through the step-by-step process of setting up a Quarto blog project, including customizing your blog's appearance and creating your first post. We covered key concepts such as YAML headers, code folding, and the importance of SEO-friendly post titles. We also learned how to preview your blog locally and make adjustments before publishing. Finally, we delved into the process of setting up a GitHub account, creating a repository for your blog, and using GitHub Desktop to easily publish and update your site. Remember, consistency in posting and quality of analysis are key to building an audience and showcasing your skills.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 28: Make your own blog
description: Learn how to show what you can do on the internet for an audience.
text: - Quarto: An open-source scientific and technical publishing system built on Pandoc that can create dynamic content with Python, R, Julia, and Observable. - YAML: A human-readable data serialization format used for configuration files and in data storage or transmission, often used in Quarto for metadata and settings. - Static Site Generator: A tool that generates a full static HTML website based on raw data and a set of templates, which Quarto functions as for blogs. - GitHub: A web-based platform and cloud-based service for version control and collaboration using Git. - Repository: A storage location for a project that contains all of its files and each file's revision history on GitHub. - GitHub Pages: A static site hosting service that takes HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files straight from a repository on GitHub and publishes a website. Markdown: A lightweight markup language with plain text formatting syntax, used in Quarto for writing content. - Git: A distributed version control system for tracking changes in source code during software development. - Clone: The process of creating a local copy of a GitHub repository on your computer. - Commit: A Git action that saves changes to your local repository, preparing them to be pushed to the remote repository on GitHub.

description: Learn how to clear out some junk that data providers give you.
text: The goal of this lesson is to introduce you to essential text cleaning techniques for sports data analysis. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand how to handle common data inconsistencies and formatting issues that often arise when working with sports statistics. You'll learn how to use regular expressions, the gsub() function, and tidyr's separate() function to clean player names, split compound data fields, and convert text-based measurements into numeric values. These skills will enable you to transform messy, raw data into clean, analysis-ready datasets. By mastering these techniques, you'll be able to produce more accurate analyses and create more professional-looking visualizations, enhancing the quality and credibility of your sports analytics blog posts.

description: Learn how to clear out some junk that data providers give you.
text: On occasion, you'll get some data from someone that ... isn't quite what you need it to be. There's something flawed in it. Some extra text, some choice that the data provider made that you just don't agree with. There's a ton of tools in the tidyverse to fix this, and you already have some tools in your toolboxt. Let's take a look at a couple. First, you know what you need. Second: The trick to this is thinking logically. If I could do X, I could get this where I want it to be. How do I do X? If you can get to a point where you ask that question, text cleaning gets a lot easier. Now, two examples.

description: Learn how to clear out some junk that data providers give you.
text: Throughout this class, we've used data from Sports Reference. If you've used their Share > CSV method to copy data from a table, you may have noticed some extra cruft in the player name field. If you haven't seen it, I'll give you an example -- a dataset of NBA players and their advanced metrics. Load it. Let's take a look: You can see that every player's name is their name, then two backslashes, then some version of their name that must have meaning to Sports Reference, but not to us. We need to get rid of that, or our graphics will look like garbage. To do this, we're going to use a little regular expression magic. Regular expressions are a programmatic way to find any pattern in text. What we're looking for is that `\\` business. But, that presents a problem, because the `\` is a special character. It's called an escape character. That escape character means what comes next is potentially special. For instance, if you see `\n`, that's a newline character. So normally, if you see that, it would add a return. So for us to get rid of the `\` we're going to have to escape the escape character with an escape character. And we have two of them. So we have to do it twice. Yes. Really. So if we wanted to find two backslashes, we need `\\\\`. Then, using regular expressions, we can say "and then everything else after this" with this: .* No really. That's it. So we're looking for `\\\\.*`. That'll find two backslashes and then everything after it. If you think this is hard ... you're right. Regular expressions are an entire month of a programming course by themselves. They are EXTREMELY powerful.
code:
nbaplayers <- read_csv("https://mattwaite.github.io/sportsdatafiles/nbaplayers.csv")
head(nbaplayers)

description: Learn how to clear out some junk that data providers give you.
section: Exercise 1: Using regular expressions to clean out junk
text: To find something in text, we'll use a function called `gsub`. The pattern in `gsub` is `pattern, what we want to replace it with, what column this can all be found in`. So in our example, the pattern is `\\\\.*`, what we want to replace it with is ... nothing, and this is all in the Player column. Just like that, the trash is gone.
code: nbaplayers |> mutate(Player=gsub("____","",____))

description: Learn how to clear out some junk that data providers give you.
text: Text cleaning is really just a set of logic puzzles. What do I need to do? How can I get there step by step? The NCAA does some very interesting things with data, making it pretty useless. Let's import it and take a look. And we'll take a look. First things first, Player isn't just player, it's player, school and conference, all in one. And Ht is a character field -- and in feet and inches. So ... this is a mess. But there is a pattern. See it? A comma after the player's name. The Conference is in parens. We can use that. For this, we're going to use a `tidyr` function called `separate` to split columns into multiple columns based on a character. We'll do this step by step.
code:
kills <- read_csv("https://mattwaite.github.io/sportsdatafiles/killsperset.csv")
head(kills)

description: Learn how to clear out some junk that data providers give you.
text: Let's use that comma to split the player and the rest. With separate, the pattern is separate WHAT, into WHAT, by WHAT. So we're going to separate Player into Player and School, and we're going to separate it by the comma. Good start. Now, let's get the conference separated.
code: kills |> separate(Player, into=c("____", "____"), sep=",")

description: Learn how to clear out some junk that data providers give you.
section: Exericse 3: Separating again, this time with a special character
text: A problem is going to crop up here -- the paren is a special character, so we have to escape it with the `\\`. Uh oh. Says we have problems in rows 15, 42 and 83. What are they? The NCAA has decided to put (FL), (NY) and (PA) into three teams to tell you they're in Florida, New York and Pennsylvania respectively.
code:
kills |> 
  separate(Player, into=c("____", "____"), sep=",") |>
  separate(School, into=c("School", "Conference"), sep="____(")

description: Learn how to clear out some junk that data providers give you.
text: Well, we can fix that with some gsub and we'll use a switch called `fixed`, which when set to TRUE it means this literal string, no special characters. One last thing: see the trailing paren?
code:
kills |> 
  separate(Player, into=c("____", "____"), sep=",") |> 
  mutate(School = gsub("(FL)", "FL", School, fixed=TRUE)) |>
  mutate(School = gsub("(NY)", "NY", School, fixed=TRUE)) |>
  mutate(School = gsub("(PA)", "PA", School, fixed=TRUE)) |>
  separate(School, into=c("School", "Conference"), sep="____(")

description: Learn how to clear out some junk that data providers give you.
section: Exercise 5: One last bit of junk
text: Believe it or not the trailing paren is NOT a special character. We can use mutate and gsub to replace ) with nothing. Looking good, no errors.
code:
kills |> 
  separate(Player, into=c("____", "____"), sep=",") |> 
  mutate(School = gsub("(FL)", "FL", School, fixed=TRUE)) |>
  mutate(School = gsub("(NY)", "NY", School, fixed=TRUE)) |>
  mutate(School = gsub("(PA)", "PA", School, fixed=TRUE)) |>
  separate(School, into=c("School", "Conference"), sep="____(") |> 
  mutate(Conference=gsub("____", "", Conference))

description: Learn how to clear out some junk that data providers give you.
text: Now, what should we do about Ht? 6-2 is not going to tell me much when I want to run a regression of height to kills per set. And it's a character field. So we need to convert it to numbers. Separate again comes to the rescue. But how do we turn that into a height? Math! Check the last mutate. And now, in 10 lines of code, using separate, mutate and gsub, we've turned the mess that is the NCAA's data into actually useful data we can analyze. These patterns of thought come in handy when facing messed up data.
code:
kills |> 
  separate(Player, into=c("Player", "School"), sep=",") |> 
  mutate(School = gsub("(FL)", "FL", School, fixed=TRUE)) |>
  mutate(School = gsub("(NY)", "NY", School, fixed=TRUE)) |>
  mutate(School = gsub("(PA)", "PA", School, fixed=TRUE)) |>
  separate(School, into=c("School", "Conference"), sep="\\(") |> 
  mutate(Conference=gsub(")", "", Conference)) |>
  separate(Ht, into=c("Feet", "Inches"), sep="-") |>
  mutate(Feet = as.numeric(Feet), Inches = as.numeric(Inches))
kills |> 
  separate(Player, into=c("Player", "School"), sep=",") |> 
  mutate(School = gsub("(FL)", "FL", School, fixed=TRUE)) |>
  mutate(School = gsub("(NY)", "NY", School, fixed=TRUE)) |>
  mutate(School = gsub("(PA)", "PA", School, fixed=TRUE)) |>
  separate(School, into=c("School", "Conference"), sep="\\(") |> 
  mutate(Conference=gsub(")", "", Conference)) |>
  separate(Ht, into=c("Feet", "Inches"), sep="-") |>
  mutate(Feet = as.numeric(Feet), Inches = as.numeric(Inches)) |>
  mutate(Height = (Feet*12)+Inches)

description: Learn how to clear out some junk that data providers give you.
text: In this lesson, we've explored various techniques for cleaning and refining sports data to make it more suitable for analysis and visualization. We started by addressing common issues in data from sources like Sports Reference, learning how to use regular expressions with gsub() to remove unwanted text from player names. We then tackled more complex data cleaning challenges using NCAA volleyball statistics, where we applied the separate() function to split compound fields into distinct columns for player names, schools, and conferences. We also learned how to handle special characters and inconsistencies in data formatting, such as state abbreviations in parentheses. Finally, we converted text-based height measurements into numeric values, demonstrating how to transform data types for quantitative analysis. These skills are crucial for preparing clean, consistent datasets that will yield accurate insights and professional-looking visualizations in your sports analytics work. Remember, effective data cleaning is often the first and most important step in any data analysis project, setting the foundation for all subsequent analyses and visualizations.

description: Learn how to clear out some junk that data providers give you.
text: - Regular Expression (Regex): A sequence of characters that defines a search pattern, used for pattern matching with strings. - `gsub()`: A function in R used for finding and replacing patterns in strings. - `separate()`: A function from the tidyr package used to split a single column into multiple columns. - Escape Character: A character (usually a backslash ) used to indicate that the following character should be interpreted literally. - `as.numeric()`: A function used to convert other data types to numeric values. - `fixed = TRUE`: An argument in gsub() that treats the pattern as a fixed string rather than a regular expression.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 30: The importance of text
description: Learn how to add important labels and annotations to your charts.
text: The goal of this lesson is to emphasize the critical role of text elements in creating effective and professional data visualizations for sports analytics. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand the importance of well-crafted headlines, informative subtitles (chatter), and strategic annotations in enhancing your charts and graphs. You'll learn how to write compelling headlines that capture the essence of your data story, create subtitles that provide context, and use annotations to guide viewers' attention to key insights. We'll apply these concepts to real sports data visualizations, demonstrating how thoughtful text can transform a basic chart into a powerful communication tool. This lesson will sharpen your ability to convey complex sports analytics concepts clearly and engagingly, making your blog posts more impactful and accessible to a wider audience.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 30: The importance of text
description: Learn how to add important labels and annotations to your charts.
text: These are the pieces of a good graphic: * Headline * Chatter * The main body * Annotations * Labels * Source line * Credit line The first on that list is the first for a reason. The headline is an incredibly important part of any graphic: it's often the first thing a reader will see. It's got to entice people in, tell them a little bit about what they're going to see, and help tell the story. The second item is the chatter -- the text underneath that headline. It needs to work with the headline to further the story, drive people toward the point, maybe add some context. The two bits of text are extremely important. Let's set up a chart and talk about how to do it wrong and how to do it better. The data and the chart code isn't important for you to follow along. The code is nothing special -- it's taken straight out of the bubble charts lesson. Under the hood, I've loaded up football games from this season and calculated yards per play on offense and defense as well as total wins for every team in college football. I've also filtered out the Big Ten into a dataframe. Let's bring up the bubble chart with Big Ten teams labeled as a starting place. This chart is missing major parts -- required for this class. To fix this, we add labels -- `labs`. Each graphic -- regardless of type --needs a title, subtitle, caption, x and y. Your title is your headline. The subtitle is called chatter -- a sentence under the headline that explains a little about the graphic. The caption is where you'll put the source of your data and the credit line -- your name. x and y should be in each `labs`, but sometimes what x and y are is obvious and you can leave them blank. For example: A bar chart with teams on the y axis doesn't need to label them as Teams. It's obvious by the rest of the chart. First, let's start with some headline basics: * Your headline should be about what the chart is about, **not what makes up the chart**. What story is the chart telling? What made it interesting to you? Don't tell me what the stats are, tell me what it says. * Your headline should be specific. **Generic headlines are boring and ignored**. * Your headline should, most often, have a verb. It's not a 100 percent requirement, but a headline without a verb means you're trying to be cute and ... * Your headline shouldn't be overly cute. Trying to get away with slang, a very Of The Moment cultural reference that will be forgotten soon, or some inside joke is asking for trouble. * Your headline should provoke a reaction. Given our graph, here's a few that don't work. The problems here: * No verb. * Generic, forgettable, doesn't say anything. * What is this chart about? What does it say? We have no real idea from the headline and chatter. * Don't repeat words from the headline in the chatter. Nebraska Nebraska looks bad. Make one of the Huskers if you're going to do this. Another example: What's wrong here? * The headline is about the stats, not the story. * The headline lacks a verb. * The headline lacks any interest, really. * The headline at least moves in the direction of what this chart is about, but see the previous two. * The chatter adds more flavor to it, but what does "the good part of the graph" even mean? We're leaving the reader with a lot of questions and no real answers. That's bad. Let's try to do this better. What works here: * Makes a strong statement. * Headline tells the story. Chatter bolsters it. * Doesn't repeat Nebraska or Huskers. Taking time to sharpen your headlines will make your graphics better.
code:
ggplot() + 
  geom_point(
    data=ypp, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, size=TotalWins), 
    color="grey", 
    alpha=.3) + 
  geom_point(
    data=bigten, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, size=TotalWins), 
    color="red") + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = 5.863151) + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 5.448228) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data=bigten, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, label=Team)
  )
ggplot() + 
  geom_point(
    data=ypp, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, size=TotalWins), 
    color="grey", 
    alpha=.3) + 
  geom_point(
    data=bigten, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, size=TotalWins), 
    color="red") + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = 5.863151) + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 5.448228) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data=bigten, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, label=Team)
  ) + 
  labs(
    x="Offensive yards per play", 
    y="Defensive yards per play", 
    title="Headline here", 
    subtitle="This is the chatter. It is chatter. Chatter.", 
    caption="Source: Data Provider | By Your Name"
    )
ggplot() + 
  geom_point(
    data=ypp, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, size=TotalWins), 
    color="grey", 
    alpha=.3) + 
  geom_point(
    data=bigten, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, size=TotalWins), 
    color="red") + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = 5.863151) + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 5.448228) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data=bigten, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, label=Team)
  ) + 
  labs(
    x="Offensive yards per play", 
    y="Defensive yards per play", 
    title="Nebraska's season", 
    subtitle="Nebraska won three games.", 
    caption="Source: Data Provider | By Your Name"
    )
ggplot() + 
  geom_point(
    data=ypp, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, size=TotalWins), 
    color="grey", 
    alpha=.3) + 
  geom_point(
    data=bigten, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, size=TotalWins), 
    color="red") + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = 5.863151) + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 5.448228) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data=bigten, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, label=Team)
  ) + 
  labs(
    x="Offensive yards per play", 
    y="Defensive yards per play", 
    title="Offensive yards per play vs defensive yards per play", 
    subtitle="Nebraska is in the good part of the graph but had a bad season.", 
    caption="Source: Data Provider | By Your Name"
    )
ggplot() + 
  geom_point(
    data=ypp, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, size=TotalWins), 
    color="grey", 
    alpha=.3) + 
  geom_point(
    data=bigten, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, size=TotalWins), 
    color="red") + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = 5.863151) + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 5.448228) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data=bigten, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, label=Team)
  ) + 
  labs(
    x="Offensive yards per play", 
    y="Defensive yards per play", 
    title="Nebraska was good, except where it mattered", 
    subtitle="The Huskers offense and defense, on a per play basis, were among the elite in the sport.", 
    caption="Source: Data Provider | By Your Name"
    )

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 30: The importance of text
description: Learn how to add important labels and annotations to your charts.
text: Another important part of a chart -- but not every chart -- is annotation. Sometimes, you need to label something in a chart to help the reader out. It can be a point or series of points. It can be a circle -- like we did in the encircling lesson. Or it can be regions of a chart. Let's return to our bubble chart. Annotations also help us draw attention to things, or help the reader understand what they're looking at. I have lines for the average offensive yards per play and average defensive yards per play -- proxies for quality. If your team averages more yards per play on offense, that's good. If they average fewer yards per play on defense, that too is good. So that sets up a situation where we have four corners, anchored by good at both and bad at both. The averages will create lines to divide those four corners up. To label them, I'm going to use geom_text and I'm just going to put numbers in the x and the y to move them where I need them. I can get those numbers by looking at the x and y axis of my chart and guessing. After that, it's increasing or decreasing the number, depending on which direction I want to move it around. Note here -- I'm only labeling two sections of the chart. There is such a thing as too much. We want to tell the story here with the most efficient use of elements -- text, color, shape.
code:
ggplot() + 
  geom_point(
    data=ypp, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, size=TotalWins), 
    color="grey", 
    alpha=.3) + 
  geom_point(
    data=bigten, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, size=TotalWins), 
    color="red") + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = 5.863151) + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 5.448228) +
  geom_text(aes(x=7, y=4.2, label="Good Offense, Good Defense"), color="blue") +
  geom_text(aes(x=5, y=6.6, label="Bad Defense, Bad Offense"), color="blue") +
  geom_text_repel(
    data=bigten, 
    aes(x=OffensiveYPP, y=DefensiveYPP, label=Team)
  ) + 
  labs(
    x="Offensive yards per play", 
    y="Defensive yards per play", 
    title="Nebraska was good, except where it mattered", 
    subtitle="The Huskers offense and defense, on a per play basis, were among the elite in the sport.", 
    caption="Source: Data Provider | By Your Name"
    )

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 30: The importance of text
description: Learn how to add important labels and annotations to your charts.
text: In this lesson, we've explored the crucial role of text elements in elevating the quality and effectiveness of sports data visualizations. We started by examining the key components of a good graphic, including headlines, chatter, annotations, and source lines. We then focused on crafting compelling headlines, learning to avoid generic titles and instead create specific, verb-driven headlines that provoke interest and convey the core message of our data. We practiced writing informative chatter that complements the headline and provides essential context. Through hands-on examples with college football data, we saw how well-chosen text can transform a basic chart into a story-driven visualization. We also delved into the strategic use of annotations, learning how to guide the viewer's eye to important data points or trends. By mastering these techniques, you'll be able to create more engaging, informative, and professional-looking visualizations for your sports analytics blog, effectively communicating complex insights to your audience.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 30: The importance of text
description: Learn how to add important labels and annotations to your charts.
text: - Headline: The main title of a data visualization that captures the key message or story of the graphic. - Chatter: The subtitle or explanatory text below the headline that provides additional context or details about the visualization. - Annotation: Text or graphical elements added to a chart to highlight or explain specific data points or trends. - `labs()`: A ggplot2 function used to add labels, titles, and captions to a visualization. - `geom_text()`: A ggplot2 function used to add text directly to a plot at specified coordinates. - `geom_text_repel()`: A function from the ggrepel package that adds non-overlapping text labels to a plot. - Caption: Text at the bottom of a graphic that typically includes the data source and credit information. - Axis Labels: Text that describes what the x and y axes represent in a chart.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 31: Using color the right way
description: Learn how the limited use of color can draw attention and focus your story.
text: The goal of this lesson is to introduce you to the strategic use of color in sports data visualizations. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand how to effectively use color to enhance your charts and graphs, drawing attention to key data points and reinforcing your story. You'll learn the basic rules of color usage in data visualization, including limiting color palette, using contrast for emphasis, and avoiding color abuse. We'll apply these concepts to real sports data, demonstrating how thoughtful color choices can transform a basic chart into a compelling visual narrative. This lesson will sharpen your ability to create more professional and impactful visualizations for your sports analytics blog, helping you guide your audience's attention and effectively communicate complex insights through color.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 31: Using color the right way
description: Learn how the limited use of color can draw attention and focus your story.
text: The main focus of this whole class -- indeed the whole sports media major -- is to tell a story. If the chart is not telling a story, then it doesn't serve a purpose and we've failed. And if our chart does tell a story, but the reader can't find it, then that means we've still failed the main purpose. Some charts do a lot of work showing the reader what the story is before we even anything to it. Some need more help. One way we can offer that help is to use color. Color can be very powerful. It can also ruin a graphic. And the right use of color isn't science -- it's art. That means color has been argued about for centuries, even in the world of graphics. The basic rules of color we're going to use are: 1. Limit the number of colors. The fewer the better. If everything is a color, nothing is a color. 2. Use contrasting colors to draw attention. A color in a sea of grey stands out. 3. Don't abuse color. Choose appropriate colors, avoid the defaults. Where do these rules come from? Experience of people who have made graphics before. Looking at what has succeeded and what has failed. **Rule 1:** Alberto Cairo, a professor in the University of Miami school of Communication and expert in data visualization, wrote in his book The Functional Art that limiting color was "not just a minimalist aesthetic choice, but a practical one. Limiting the amount of colors and different fonts in your graphics will help you create a sense of unity in the composition." He went on: "The best way to disorient your readers is to fill your graphic with objects colored in pure accent tones. Pure colors are uncommon in nature, so limit them to highlight whatever is important in your graphics, and use subdued hues — grays, light blues, and greens — for everything else. In other words: If you know that the brain prioritizes what it pays attention to, prioritize beforehand." **Rule 2:** Swiss cartographer Eduard Imhof wrote in 1982 his first rule of of color composition: "Pure, bright or very strong colors have loud unbearable effects when they stand unrelieved over large areas adjacent to each other, but extraordinary effects can be achieved when they are used sparingly on or between dull background tones." **Rule 3:** Edward Tufte, in Envisioning Information, wrote that adding color was the easy part. "The often scant benefits derived from coloring data indicate that putting a good color in a good place is a complex matter. Indeed, so difficult and subtle that avoiding catastrophe becomes the first principle in bringing color to information: Above all, do no harm."

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 31: Using color the right way
description: Learn how the limited use of color can draw attention and focus your story.
text: We're going to build a simple bar chart and layer in come color as an example. Let's look at Big Ten teams in the 2025 NCAA Tournament. So let's explore that. First load the tidyverse. And the data. We're going to do four things here: first, we'll load in the season stats for each team in college basketball in 2024-2025. Then we'll filter out Big Ten teams to get us a dataframe of 18 teams called `big`. We'll then filter B1G teams that made the tournament. Lastly, we'll make a dataframe of just Michigan State, who seem to always be the last Big Ten team in the tournament. And we can see that `big` data with head. The `dance` and mi` dataframes have the same columns.
code:
stats <- read_csv("https://mattwaite.github.io/sportsdatafiles/stats25.csv")

teams <- c("Nebraska", "Iowa", "Northwestern", "Minnesota", "Wisconsin", "Illinois", "Indiana", "Purdue", "Michigan", "Michigan State", "Ohio State", "Penn State", "Rutgers", "Maryland", "Oregon", "Washington", "USC", "UCLA")

tournament <- c("Illinois", "Maryland", "Michigan", "Michigan State", "Oregon", "Purdue", "UCLA", "Wisconsin")

big <- stats |> filter(school %in% teams)

dance <- stats |> filter(school %in% tournament)

mi <- stats |> filter(school == "Michigan State")
head(big)

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 31: Using color the right way
description: Learn how the limited use of color can draw attention and focus your story.
text: Let's start by making a simple bar chart of `srs`. It's just a simple rating of each team. We're going to re-order it and coord_flip it like we've done before. Now we've got a base.
code:
ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=big, aes(x=reorder(school, ____), weight=____)) + 
  coord_flip()

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 31: Using color the right way
description: Learn how the limited use of color can draw attention and focus your story.
section: Exercise 2: Using color to reduce attention
text: Let's make that base fade into the background by changing the color to light grey (which is one word in ggplot). With bar charts, we're changing the `fill`. Changing the `color` will only change the outline of the bar. Now we can add layers.
code:
ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=big, aes(x=reorder(school, ____), weight=____), fill="____") + 
  coord_flip()

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 31: Using color the right way
description: Learn how the limited use of color can draw attention and focus your story.
text: Now we're going to add in `dance` and `mi` to our chart. We want them to stand out from the rest of the B1G, so we're going to made `dance` dark grey (again, one word) and we'll use Michigan State's hex code to shade them in. See how you can now see who the tournament teams were, and where Michigan State ranked in the conference? Odd to see they were the last team out of the tournament with much higher-rated teams, no? That's often where stories lie. We've got one last color-based thing to do -- get rid of the grey background.
code:
ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=big, aes(x=reorder(school, srs), weight=srs), fill="____") + 
  geom_bar(data=dance, aes(x=reorder(school, srs), weight=srs), fill="____") +  
  geom_bar(data=____, aes(x=reorder(school, srs), weight=srs), fill="#18453B") +    
  coord_flip()

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 31: Using color the right way
description: Learn how the limited use of color can draw attention and focus your story.
section: Exercise 4: Getting out of the way of our colors
text: `ggplot` by default adds a grey background to every chart. I don't know why, but it's there. That grey takes away from our contrast and makes the reader's eye wander more. We want to draw attention to our shapes, and use color to draw the eye to the shape we want them to see. Something that impacts that is bad, so we want to get rid of it. The fastest way is to use pre-made themes. The two that come in the most handy are `theme_minimal` and `theme_light`, both of which ditch the grey background. Let's use `theme_minimal` here. This chart has some work left -- headlines, some text to explain the dark grey bars, the axis labels are garbage -- but this chart tells a story and our reader can find it.
code:
ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=big, aes(x=reorder(school, srs), weight=srs), fill="____") + 
  geom_bar(data=dance, aes(x=reorder(school, srs), weight=srs), fill="____") +  
  geom_bar(data=____, aes(x=reorder(school, srs), weight=srs), fill="#18453B") +  
  ____() + 
  coord_flip()

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 31: Using color the right way
description: Learn how the limited use of color can draw attention and focus your story.
text: In this lesson, we've explored the powerful role of color in enhancing sports data visualizations. We began by discussing the three fundamental rules of color usage: limiting the number of colors, using contrast to draw attention, and avoiding color abuse. We then applied these principles to a real-world example using college basketball data from the NCAA tournament. Through hands-on exercises, we transformed a basic bar chart into a visually compelling story about Big Ten teams' performance. We practiced using color strategically to fade less important data into the background, highlight key information, and guide the viewer's eye to the most crucial elements of our visualization. We also learned how to remove distracting elements like default backgrounds to further emphasize our color choices. By mastering these techniques, you'll be able to create more effective, professional-looking visualizations that not only present data but tell a clear, visually engaging story for your sports analytics blog.

text: - Color Contrast: The use of different colors to make certain elements stand out from others in a visualization. - Color Palette: A set of colors chosen to be used together in a visualization, often selected for their harmony and effectiveness in conveying information. - `fill`: A ggplot2 parameter used to set the interior color of graphical elements like bars in a bar chart. - `color`: A ggplot2 parameter that sets the outline color of graphical elements. - Hex Code: A six-digit code that represents a specific color in digital design, often used for precise color selection in visualizations.

description: Learn how to add the final visual flourishes to make your charts professional and presentable.
text: The goal of this lesson is to teach you how to apply professional finishing touches to your sports data visualizations, elevating them from basic charts to publication-ready graphics. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand how to refine your ggplot2 charts with custom themes, appropriate font sizes, and strategic use of white space. You'll learn to adjust title positions, modify grid lines, and fine-tune text elements to create a visual hierarchy that guides the reader's eye. We'll also cover special considerations for waffle charts and how to properly label multi-chart visualizations. Additionally, you'll understand the importance of consistent styling across your graphics and how to implement code folding in your blog posts. These skills will help you create polished, professional-looking visualizations that effectively communicate your sports analytics insights and enhance the overall quality of your blog.

description: Learn how to add the final visual flourishes to make your charts professional and presentable.
text: The output from ggplot is good, but not great. We need to add some pieces to it. The elements of a good graphic are: * Headline * Chatter * The main body * Annotations * Labels * Source line * Credit line That looks like: Let's explore fixing up ggplot's output. We'll need a graphic to work with first, so I'm going to use a bubble chart. The first thing you should do with any chart that isn't a waffle chart -- more on that later -- is remove the default plot look and grey backgound by adding a pre-defined theme. There are two you should use to clean your charts up: `theme_minimal` or `theme_light`. These pre-packaged themes do just that. They make your chart cleaner and easier to read in one swoop. **NOTE**: Adding a `theme()` is something you do last. And if you're going to change theme elements, you need to **apply your prepackaged theme first**, then do your theme customization. Everything in ggplot is rendered in order. If you do custom theming, THEN apply the prepackaged theme, the prepackaged theme will overwrite anything you did. So `theme_minimal()` or `theme_light()` comes first. Scroll down here and find the theme_minimal() at the very end. We're just adding it on, like any other part of ggplot. Compare that to your previous chart. Easier to read, right? We're off to a good start, but our text has no real hierarchy. We want our headline to stand out more. So let's change that. When it comes to changing text, the place to do that is in the theme element. [There are a lot of ways to modify the theme](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/theme.html). We'll start easy. Let's make the headline bigger and bold. Scroll down here under theme_minimal and you'll see what I've done. Now let's fix a few other things -- like the axis labels being too big, the subtitle could be smaller and lets drop some grid lines. Scroll down and take a look. One last thing: ggplot, by default, sets the margin for the title and subtitle to the edge of the chart area -- the area were the data appears. It creates an odd spacing where the title is shifted right for little reason. We can fix that too in `theme()`.
