description: Learn how to add the final visual flourishes to make your charts professional and presentable.
section: Waffle charts require some special attention
text: Frequently in my classes, students use the waffle charts library quite extensively to make graphics. This is a quick walkthough on how to get a waffle chart into a publication ready state. Let's look back to the softball waffle chart we did in the waffle tutorial that had lots of waffles in them. That one requires the most work, but note two things: 1. This is still just a ggplot chart, so everything in the first part of this tutorial applies. 2. This is a facet chart, so everything done here in theme applies to them too, regardless of the chart type. We can add labels and themes, but you have to be careful: for a waffle chart to look like a waffle chart, we used `theme_void()` which works similarly to `theme_minimal()` in that it applies a bunch of choices to your chart. But in this case, we want to undo one thing theme void does and that is remove the x-axis label. Strangely, if you mess with one axis label, *both* return. So how do we get the x-axis back but not the y? We tell it to make the y blank, and it doing so, it brings the x axis label back from the dead. Note below that labels look the same as the other charts, but what we put in x is an explanation of what each square represents. This might be yards, or games, or homeruns or whatever. But we need to tell our audience what a square is. Next: we can rename our legend in labs by specifying what the `fill` or `color` label is. In this case, we'll make it "Record" since we're talking about wins and losses. Then, in our theme, note two things: - `axis.title.y = element_blank()` just drops the y-axis label from appearing. It would only say "y" in this case anyway, so it's not necessary. - `strip.text.x = element_text(size = 6, hjust=0)` changes the top labels on each chart (aka the column that was faceted). In this case, it's the name of the school. In ggplot that label is called a strip, so strip.text.x is the x axis strip. The size makes it smaller and hjust is the horizontal justification. For hjust, 0 is left, 1 is right. Left looks better to me. Note: These finishing touches aren't optional. They're required of every graphic you create for this course.
code:
ggplot() +
  geom_tile(
    data=team_waffle, 
    aes(
      x = x, y = y, fill = category
      ), 
    color = "white", 
    linewidth = .5) +
  facet_wrap(~team, ncol = 6) +
  coord_equal() + 
  theme_void() + 
  labs(
    x = "1 square = 1 game",
    title="Big 10 Softball: The Haves and Have Nots", 
    subtitle="UCLA and Oregon made the College World Series, but Nebraska came close.",
    caption="Source: NCAA | Graphic by Matt Waite",
    fill = "Record") + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.title.y = element_blank(),
    strip.text.x = element_text(size = 6, hjust=0)
  )

description: Learn how to add the final visual flourishes to make your charts professional and presentable.
text: In this lesson, we've explored the crucial final steps in creating professional-quality sports data visualizations. We started by reviewing the essential elements of a good graphic, including headlines, chatter, annotations, and source lines. We then delved into practical techniques for refining ggplot2 charts, such as applying theme_minimal() to create a clean base, customizing text elements for better visual hierarchy, and adjusting margins for improved layout. We learned how to strategically modify grid lines and axis labels to enhance readability without cluttering the visualization. Special attention was given to waffle charts, demonstrating how to add appropriate labels and themes while working within the constraints of theme_void. Throughout the lesson, we emphasized the importance of consistent styling and the necessity of including these finishing touches in all graphics for this course. Finally, we highlighted the requirement of implementing code folding in blog posts, ensuring that your visualizations are not only visually appealing but also presented in a professional, interactive format. These skills will set your sports analytics blog apart, demonstrating a level of polish and attention to detail that marks the transition from amateur to professional-quality data visualization.

description: Learn how to add the final visual flourishes to make your charts professional and presentable.
text: - `theme_minimal()`: A ggplot2 function that applies a clean, minimalist theme to charts, removing unnecessary visual elements. - `theme()`: A ggplot2 function used to customize various aspects of a plot's appearance, including text, lines, and backgrounds. - `element_text()`: A function used within theme() to specify text properties such as size, color, and font face. - `plot.title.position`: A theme element that controls the alignment of the plot title, often set to "plot" for left alignment. - `panel.grid.minor`: A theme element used to control the appearance of minor grid lines in a plot. - `labs()`: A ggplot2 function used to add or modify plot labels, including title, subtitle, and axis labels. - `code-fold`: A YAML header option in Quarto documents that allows readers to show or hide code chunks in the final output. - Visual Hierarchy: The arrangement of graphic elements to show their order of importance, guiding the viewer's attention through the visualization.

description: Learn how to scrape tables from websites.
text: The goal of this lesson is to introduce you to web scraping using the rvest package in R. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand how to extract data from HTML tables on websites and import it into R for analysis. You'll learn how to identify the correct HTML elements to target, use XPath to select specific table data, and handle common challenges like unnamed columns and multi-row headers. We'll apply these techniques to real sports statistics websites, scraping college football and basketball data. You'll also practice cleaning and combining data from multiple years. This skill will allow you to automate data collection from web sources, saving time and enabling more up-to-date analysis of sports statistics that may not be available through official APIs or datasets.

description: Learn how to scrape tables from websites.
text: All the way back in Lesson 2, we used Google Sheets and importHTML to get our own data out of a website. For me, that's a lot of pointing and clicking and copying and pasting. R has a library that can automate the harvesting of data from HTML on the internet. It's called `rvest`. Let's grab [a simple, basic HTML table from College Football Stats](https://cfbstats.com/2025/leader/national/team/offense/split01/category09/sort01.html). There's nothing particularly strange about this table -- it's simply formatted and easy to scrape. First we'll need some libraries. We're going to use a library called `rvest`, which you already have installed. But like always, if you are having problems, you can install it by running `install.packages('rvest')` in the console. The rvest package has functions that make fetching, reading and parsing HTML simple. The first thing we need to do is specify a url that we're going to scrape. Now, the most difficult part of scraping data from any website is knowing what exact HTML tag you need to grab. In this case, we want a `<table>` tag that has all of our data table in it. But how do you tell R which one that is? Well, it's easy, once you know what to do. But it's not simple. So I've made a short video to show you how to find it. When you have simple tables, the code is very simple. You create a variable to receive the data, then pass it the url, read the html that was fetched, find the node you need using your XPath value you just copied and you tell rvest that it's a table.
code: scoringoffenseurl <- "https://cfbstats.com/2025/leader/national/team/offense/split01/category09/sort01.html"

description: Learn how to scrape tables from websites.
section: Exercise 1: Copy the XPath and scrape
text: Watch the video, go to the URL we're scraping and copy the XPath for the table. Paste it into the code below. What we get from this is ... not a dataframe. It's a list with one element in it, which just so happens to be our dataframe. When you get this, the solution is simple: just write to a new variable with the first list element. And what do we have? We have data, ready for analysis.
code:
scoringoffense <- scoringoffenseurl |>
  read_html() |>
  html_nodes(xpath = '____') |>
  html_table()
scoring <- scoringoffense[[1]]
head(scoring)

description: Learn how to scrape tables from websites.
text: What if we want more than one year in our dataframe? This is a common problem. What if we want to look at every scoring offense going back several years? The website has them going back to 2009. How can we combine them? First, we should note, that the data does not have anything in it to indicate what year it comes from. So we're going to have to add that. And we're going to have to figure out a way to stack two dataframes on top of each other. So let's grab 2024. First, how are we going to know, in the data, which year our data is from? We can use mutate. But we have a problem. Let's look at head again. Notice the first column -- it doesn't have a name. That's bad.
code:
scoringoffenseurl24 <- "https://cfbstats.com/2024/leader/national/team/offense/split01/category09/sort01.html"

scoringoffense24 <- scoringoffenseurl24 |>
  read_html() |>
  html_nodes(xpath = '//*[@id="content"]/div[2]/table') |>
  html_table()

scoringoffense2024 <- scoringoffense24[[1]]
head(scoringoffense2024)

description: Learn how to scrape tables from websites.
text: To fix this all of this, we're going to run two functions -- `rename` and `mutate`. The mutate is easy -- were going to mutate a new column that has the year the data comes from. Rename, to refresh your memory, is rename(NAME WE ARE GIVING THE COLUMN = WHICH COLUMN WE ARE RENAMING). But wait. Doesn't our first column not have a name? How are we going to ... you know ... name it? We can call it by it's position. In this case, it's in position 1. But we have to fix the name issue before we can fix the what-year-is-it problem. We'll also fix our 2021 data while we're at it. And now, to combine the two tables together length-wise -- we need to make long data -- we'll use a dpylr function called `bind_rows`. The good thing is bind_rows is simple: It's just `bind_rows(dataframe, otherdataframe)` and we can keep putting dataframes in there until we run out. The catch is that they have to have the same columns for it to work. In our case, we have that. Note if we run glimpse on the dataframe called combined that it has 257 rows -- which just so happens to be what the number of FBS teams added together totals for two seasons.
code:
fixedscoringoffense2024 <- scoringoffense2024 |> rename(Rank = 1) |> mutate(YEAR = ____)
fixedscoringoffense2025 <- scoring |> rename(Rank = 1) |> mutate(YEAR = ____)
combined <- bind_rows(____2024, ____2025)
glimpse(combined)

description: Learn how to scrape tables from websites.
text: What do you do when the table has non-standard headers? Unfortunately, non-standard means there's no one way to do it -- it's going to depend on the table and the headers. But here's one idea: Don't try to make it work. I'll explain. Let's try to get [college basketball season team stats from Sports Reference](https://www.sports-reference.com/cbb/seasons/2025-school-stats.html). If you look at that page, you'll see the problem right away -- the headers span two rows, and they repeat (i.e. there's conference wins, home wins and away wins, but because the conference, home and away markers are on a different line, R won't know which W is which). That's going to be all kinds of no good. **You can't import that.** Dataframes must have names all in one row. If you have two-line headers, you have a problem you have to fix before you can do anything else with it.

description: Learn how to scrape tables from websites.
text: First we'll grab the page. But this time, in our `html_table` bits, which we've just merrily been using untouched, we're going to add a switch: We're going to tell it that our table does not have headers by saying `header=FALSE`. And we'll take a look at what we have. So, that's not ideal. We have headers and data mixed together, and our columns are named X1 to X38. Also note: They're all character fields. Because the headers are interspersed with data, it all gets called character data. So we've got to first rename each field. Note, this is nothing special, it's just a lot of code and kind of tedious. I'm just going through each field and naming it what it is. While I'm at it, **I am removing any special characters, spaces or anything that would make R throw a fit.** So no dashes, percent signs, no columns that start with number. Now we have to get rid of those headers interspersed in the data. We can do this with a filter, but we need a pattern to get rid of them. Let's pull up the head of the data and see if we can spot one. From that, I can see that in my Rank column, I have a row that has "Rk" in it. I don't want that. Then, in my Games column, along with several others, I have "Overall" in a row. Don't want that either. With filters so far, we've been using mostly == and greater than/less than. The opposite of == is !=, or does not equal. In other words, keep everything EXCEPT this. And finally, we need to change the data type of all the fields that need it. We're going to use a clever little trick. We're going to use `mutate_at`, which means mutate these fields. The pattern for `mutate_at` is `mutate_at` these variables and do this thing to them. But instead of specifying which of 38 variables we're going to mutate, we're going to specify the one we don't want to change, which is just the name of the school. And we want to convert them to numeric, which is simple. One last thing: Who needs columns called Blank1, Blank2, Blank3, etc? Here's what it looks like: And just like that (cough cough), we have a method for getting up to the minute season stats for every team in Division I. While this seems like a lot of work, if your job is to analyze college basketball as the year goes by, and you're going to be pulling this page every few days, the work you just did will save you hours of time down the road. Automating the import of data is an important function for data scientists to save time on drudgery and focus on analysis.
code:
url <- "https://www.sports-reference.com/cbb/seasons/2025-school-stats.html"

stats <- url |>
  read_html() |>
  html_nodes(xpath = '//*[@id="basic_school_stats"]') |>
  html_table(header=____)
head(stats)
statsrenamed <- stats |> 
  rename(
    Rank=X1, 
    School=X2, 
    Games=X3, 
    OverallWins=X4, 
    OverallLosses=X5, 
    WinPct=X6, 
    OverallSRS=X7, 
    OverallSOS=X8, 
    Blank1=X9, 
    ConferenceWins=X10, 
    ConferenceLosses=X11, 
    Blank2=X12, 
    HomeWins=X13, 
    HomeLosses=X14, 
    Blank3=X15, 
    AwayWins=X16, 
    AwayLosses=X17, 
    Blank4=X18, 
    ForPoints=X19, 
    OppPoints=X20, 
    Blank5=X21, 
    Minutes=X22, 
    FieldGoalsMade=X23, 
    FieldGoalsAttempted=X24, 
    FieldGoalPCT=X25, 
    ThreePointMade=X26, 
    ThreePointAttempts=X27, 
    ThreePointPct=X28, 
    FreeThrowsMade=X29, 
    FreeThrowsAttempted=X30, 
    FreeThrowPCT=X31, 
    OffensiveRebounds=X32, 
    TotalRebounds=X33, 
    Assists=X34, 
    Steals=X35, 
    Blocks=X36, 
    Turnovers=X37, 
    PersonalFouls=X38)
head(statsrenamed)
statswithoutgarbage <- statsrenamed |> 
  filter(Rank != "Rk" & Games != "Overall") 
statswithnumbers <- statswithoutgarbage |> 
  mutate_at(vars(-School), as.numeric) |> 
  select(-starts_with("Blank"))
head(statswithnumbers)

description: Learn how to scrape tables from websites.
text: In this lesson, we've explored the powerful capabilities of the rvest package for web scraping in R. We started with a simple example, extracting a basic HTML table from a college football statistics website. We then progressed to more complex scenarios, including combining data from multiple years and handling tables with non-standard headers. Throughout the tutorial, you've learned key rvest functions like read_html(), html_nodes(), and html_table(), as well as how to use XPath to target specific elements. We also covered important data cleaning steps, such as renaming columns, filtering out unwanted rows, and converting data types. By working through real-world examples with sports data, you've gained practical experience in overcoming common web scraping challenges. Remember, while web scraping can be a powerful tool for data collection, it's important to use it responsibly and in compliance with websites' terms of service. With these skills, you're now equipped to automate data collection from web sources, enabling more efficient and up-to-date sports analytics.

description: Learn how to scrape tables from websites.
text: - `rvest`: An R package designed for web scraping, providing functions to easily extract data from HTML web pages. - Web scraping: The process of automatically collecting data from websites using programming techniques. - XPath: A query language used to navigate through elements and attributes in an XML or HTML document. - `html_nodes()`: An rvest function used to select specific HTML elements from a web page based on CSS selectors or XPath. - `html_table()`: An rvest function that extracts data from HTML tables and converts them into R data frames. - `read_html()`: An rvest function that reads HTML content from a URL or file. - `bind_rows()`: A dplyr function used to combine multiple data frames vertically, stacking them on top of each other. - `mutate_at()`: A dplyr function that applies a transformation to multiple columns in a data frame. - `rename()`: A dplyr function used to change the names of columns in a data frame. - `header=FALSE`: An argument used in html_table() to indicate that the first row of the HTML table should not be treated as column names.

description: Learn how to predict something using multiple inputs.
text: The goal of this lesson is to introduce you to multiple regression, a powerful statistical technique that builds upon simple linear regression. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand how to use multiple predictors to create a more comprehensive model for analyzing sports data. We'll explore how to build and interpret multiple regression models using college football data, covering concepts such as multicollinearity and model evaluation. You'll learn how to select appropriate variables, interpret regression outputs, and use your model to make predictions. We'll also discuss the practical implications of these models in the context of sports analytics, such as predicting team performance based on various statistics. This lesson will enhance your ability to create more nuanced and accurate predictive models, a crucial skill for advanced sports data analysis.

description: Learn how to predict something using multiple inputs.
text: In an earlier lesson, we looked at correlations and linear regression to predict how one element of a game would predict the score. But we know that a single variable, in all but the rarest instances, is not going to be that predictive. We need more than one. Enter multiple regression. Multiple regression lets us add -- wait for it -- multiple predictors to our equation to help us get a better fit to reality. That presents it's own problems. So let's get set up. The dataset we'll use is all college football games since the 2011 season. We need the tidyverse and a library called `Hmisc` that we'll use later. And the data. One way to show how successful a football team was for a game is to show the differential between the team's score and the opponent's score. Score a lot more than the opponent = good, score a lot less than the opponent = bad. And, relatively speaking, the more the better. So let's create that differential. Let's also get our net yardage stat back. And because we'll need it later, let's add the turnover margin. The linear model code we used before is pretty straight forward. Its `field` is predicted by `field`. Here's a simple linear model that looks at predicting a team's point differential by looking at their net yards. Remember: There's a lot here, but only some of it we care about. What is the Adjusted R-squared value? What's the p-value and is it less than .05? In this case, we can predict 67 percent of the difference in differential with the net yardage in the game.
code:
logs <- read_csv("https://mattwaite.github.io/sportsdatafiles/footballlogs1121.csv")
difflogs <- logs |> mutate(
  Differential = TeamScore - OpponentScore, 
  NetYards = OffensiveYards - DefYards, 
  TurnoverMargin = DefTotalTurnovers - TotalTurnovers)
yards <- lm(Differential ~ NetYards, data=difflogs)
summary(yards)

description: Learn how to predict something using multiple inputs.
text: To add more predictors to this mix, we merely add them. But it's not that simple, as you'll see in a moment. So first, let's look at adding how well the turnover margin worked out for a team to our prediction model. We do that with a simple plus. First things first: What is the adjusted R-squared? Second: what is the p-value and is it less than .05? Third: Compare the residual standard error. We went from 13.12 to 10.55. The meaning of this is both really opaque and also simple -- by adding data, we reduced the amount of error in our model. Residual standard error is the total distance between what our model would predict and what we actually have in the data. So lots of residual error means the distance between reality and our model is wider. So the width of our predictive range in this example shrank while we improved the amount of the difference we could predict. That's good, and not always going to be the case. One of the more difficult things to understand about multiple regression is the issue of **multicollinearity**. What that means is that there is significant correlation overlap between two variables -- the two are related to each other as well as to the target output -- and all you are doing by adding both of them is adding error with no real value to the R-squared. In pure statistics, we don't want any multicollinearity at all. Violating that assumption limits the applicability of what you are doing. So if we have some multicollinearity, it limits our scope of application to college football. We can't say this will work for every football league and level everywhere. What we need to do is see how correlated each value is to each other and throw out ones that are highly co-correlated. Because you might be thinking, why not just include EVERYTHING. Won't that give us the best model? The answer? No. It's just not practical that you'd be able to predict every input value. You don't have God-like vision on a sport. The idea here is to make a model that is highly predictive while requiring the fewest inputs. Less stuff in, less stuff that can go wrong. To find problems with multicollinearity, we have to create a correlation matrix that shows us how each value is correlated to our outcome variable, but also with each other. We can do that in the `Hmisc` library. We installed it at the beginning, but in case you did not, you can go to the console in R Studio and run `install.packages("Hmisc")` First, we're going to remove all non-numeric values from our data. There's a whole other set of tools for creating correlations based on categorical data. For now, we're just going to remove them. To do that, we'll use a shortcut called `select_if` that selects columns if they match a criteria -- in this case, they're numeric. And then, there's two columns that are numeric but aren't numbers, per se. They're the Game number (1,2,3,4,5 and so on) and the season (2020, 2021, etc.). Those don't have any meaning, so we'll dump them too. The last select is just reordering the columns for what comes next. We'll end up with a new dataframe called simplelogs. Before we proceed, what we're looking to do in the next block is follow the Differential column down, looking for correlation values near 1 or -1. Correlations go from -1, meaning perfect negative correlation, to 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect positive correlation. So we're looking for numbers near 1 or -1 for their predictive value. BUT: We then need to see if that value is also highly correlated with something else. If it is, we have a decision to make. We get our correlation matrix like this: Notice right away -- NetYards is highly correlated to differential. But NetYards's also highly correlated with RushingYards, OffensiveYards and DefYards. And that makes sense: those things all feed into NetYards. Including all of these measures would be pointless -- they would add error without adding much in the way of predictive power.
code:
model1 <- lm(Differential ~ ???????? + ????????Margin, data=difflogs)
summary(model1)
simplelogs <- difflogs |> 
  select_if(is.numeric) |> 
  select(-Game, -Season) |> 
  select(Differential, NetYards, TurnoverMargin, everything())
cormatrix <- rcorr(as.matrix(simplelogs))

cormatrix$r

section: Exercise 2: Adding more to the model
text: We can add more just by simply adding them. Let's add the average yard per play for both offense and defense -- OffenseAvg and DefenseAvg. They're correlated to NetYards, but not as much as you might expect. Go down the list: What is the Adjusted R-squared now? What is the p-value and is it less than .05? What is the Residual standard error?

model3 <- lm(Differential ~ ???????? + ?????????Margin + ???Avg + ????????Avg, data=difflogs)
summary(model3)

description: Learn how to predict something using multiple inputs.
section: Using our model to predict things
text: The final thing we can do with this is predict things. Look at our coefficients table. See the Estimates? We can build a formula from that, same as we did with linear regressions. How does this apply in the real world? Let's pretend for a minute that you are Scott Frost, and you have a mess on your hands. Your job is to win conference titles. To do that, we need to know what attributes of a team should we emphasize. We can do that by looking at what previous Big Ten conference champions looked like. So if our goal is to predict a conference champion team, we need to know what those teams did. Here's the last four regular season Big Ten conference champions in this dataset. Now it's just plug and chug. It's the estimate times the actual number of that thing. Then just add them all up with the intercept at the end. So a team with those numbers is going to average scoring almost 19 more points per game than their opponent. Sound like Ohio State in the last three years? How does that compare to Nebraska this season? Plug and chug: By this model, it predicted we would average outscoring our opponents by a touchdown over the season. Reality? We outscored them by 5.25 on average. And we won three games. Thanks Northwestern!

  filter(Team == "Michigan" & Season == 2021 | Team == "Ohio State" & Season == 2020 | Team == "Ohio State" & Season == 2019 | Team == "Ohio State" & Season == 2018) |>

    meanNetYards = mean(NetYards),
    meanTurnoverMargin = mean(TurnoverMargin),
    meanDefAvg = mean(DefAvg),

Along the way, we covered important concepts such as multicollinearity and learned how to use correlation matrices to identify potential issues in our variable selection. description: Learn how to predict something using multiple inputs.
text: - Multiple Regression: A statistical technique that uses two or more independent variables to predict the outcome of a dependent variable. - Predictor Variable: Also known as an independent variable, it's a factor used in regression to predict the dependent variable. - Multicollinearity: A phenomenon in which two or more predictor variables in a multiple regression model are highly correlated, potentially leading to unreliable and unstable estimates of regression coefficients. - Adjusted R-squared: A modified version of R-squared that adjusts for the number of predictors in a model, providing a more accurate measure of the model's explanatory power. - p-value: In regression analysis, a measure of the probability that an observed relationship between variables occurred by chance. - Residual Standard Error: A measure of the average amount that the response variable deviates from the true regression line. - Correlation Matrix: A table showing correlation coefficients between variables, used to identify potential multicollinearity issues. - Coefficient: In a regression equation, the number that represents the rate of change of one variable as a function of changes in the other. - Intercept: The expected mean value of Y when all X variables are zero. - Model Prediction: The process of using a regression model to estimate the value of the dependent variable based on given values of the independent variables.

description: Learn how to group like things together with math.
text: The goal of this lesson is to introduce you to k-means clustering, a powerful technique for grouping similar data points in sports analytics. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand how to use clustering to identify player types and find comparable athletes based on their statistical profiles. We'll walk through the process of preparing data, determining the optimal number of clusters, and interpreting the results using college basketball player statistics. You'll learn how to apply k-means clustering to group players with similar performance metrics, visualize the clusters, and use this information to find peers for specific athletes like Keisei Tominaga. This skill will enhance your ability to conduct more sophisticated player comparisons and team composition analyses, providing valuable insights for talent evaluation and strategic decision-making in sports.

description: Learn how to group like things together with math.
text: One common effort in sports is to classify teams and players -- who are this player's peers? What teams are like this one? Who should we compare a player to? Truth is, most sports commentators use nothing more sophisticated that looking at a couple of stats or use the "eye test" to say a player is like this or that. There's better ways. In this lesson, we're going to use a method that sounds advanced but it really quite simple called k-means clustering. It's based on the concept of the k-nearest neighbor algorithm. You're probably already scared. Don't be. Imagine two dots on a scatterplot. If you took a ruler out and measured the distance between those dots, you'd know how far apart they are. In math, that's called the Euclidean distance. It's just the space between them in numbers. Where k-nearest neighbor comes in, you have lots of dots and you want measure the distance between all of them. What does k-means clustering do? It lumps them into groups based on the average distance between them. Players who are good on offense but bad on defense are over here, good offense good defense are over here. And using the Euclidean distance between them, we can decide who is in and who is out of those groups. For this exercise, I want to look at Keisei Tominaga, probably the most fun player ever on a Fred Hoiberg Nebraska team. So who does Keisei compare to? To answer this, we'll use k-means clustering. First thing we do is load some libraries and set a seed, so if we run this repeatedly, our random numbers are generated from the same base. If you don't have the cluster library, just add it on the console with `install.packages("cluster")` Let's load the player data from this season. To cluster this data properly, we have some work to do. First, it won't do to have players who haven't played, so we can use filter to find anyone with greater than 0 minutes played. Next, Keisei Tominaga is a guard, so let's just look at guards. Third, we want to limit the data to things that make sense to look at for Keisei Tominaga -- things like shooting, three point shooting, assists, turnovers and points. Now, k-means clustering doesn't work as well with data that can be on different scales. So comparing a percentage to a count metric -- shooting percentage to points -- would create chaos because shooting percentages are a fraction of 1 and points, depending on when they are in the season, could be quite large. So we have to scale each metric -- put them on a similar basis using the distance from the max value as our guide. If this sounds familiar, it is: It's a z-score. Also, k-means clustering won't work with text data, so we need to create a dataframe that's just the numbers, but scaled. We can do that with another select, and using mutate_all with the scale function. The `na.omit()` means get rid of any blanks, because they too will cause errors. With k-means clustering, we decide how many clusters we want. Most often, researchers will try a handful of different cluster numbers and see what works. But there are methods for finding the optimal number. One method is called the Elbow method. One implementation of this, [borrowed from the University of Cincinnati's Business Analytics program](https://uc-r.github.io/kmeans_clustering), does this quite nicely with a graph that will help you decide for yourself. All you need to do in this code is change out the data frame -- `playersscaled` in this case -- and run it. The Elbow method -- so named because you're looking for the "elbow" where the line flattens out. In this case, it looks like a K of 5 is ideal. So let's try that. We're going to use the kmeans function, saving it to an object called clusters. We just need to tell it our dataframe name, how many centers (k) we want, and we'll use a sensible default for how many different configurations to try. Let's look at what we get. Interpreting this output, the very first thing you need to know is that **the cluster numbers are meaningless**. They aren't ranks. They aren't anything. After you have taken that on board, look at the cluster sizes at the top. What clusters are large compared to others. That's notable. Then we can look at the cluster means. For reference, 0 is going to be average. So which groups are above average on minutes played? Which groups are below? So which group is Keisei Tominaga in? Well, first we have to put our data back together again. In clusters, there is a list of cluster assignments in the same order we put them in, but recall we have no names. So we need to re-combine them with our original data. We can do that with the following: Now we have a dataframe called playercluster that has our player names and what cluster they are in. The fastest way to find Keisei Tominaga is to double click on the playercluster table in the environment and use the search in the top right of the table. Because this is based on some random selections of points to start the groupings, these may change from person to person. We now have a dataset and can plot it like anything else. Let's get Keisei Tominaga and then plot him against the rest of college basketball on points versus minutes played. What does that look like? Let's look at scoring in a chart. Not bad, not bad. But who are Keisei Tominaga's peers? If we look at the numbers in his group, how many of them are there? What are their stats relative to average? So let's limit them to just Big Ten guards. Unfortunately, I don't have Conference names in my data, so I'm going to have to do this the hard way and make a list of Big Ten teams and filter on that. Then I'll sort by minutes played. Who are the guards most like Keisei Tominaga in the Big Ten? We should further limit our number by minutes played, but you can do that on your own another time.
code:
players <- read_csv("https://mattwaite.github.io/sportsdatafiles/players24.csv")
playersselected <- players |> 
  filter(MP>0) |> filter(Pos == "G") |> 
  select(Player, Team, Pos, MP, `FG%`, `3P%`, AST, TOV, PTS) |> 
  na.omit() 
playersscaled <- playersselected |> 
  select(MP, `FG%`, `3P%`, AST, TOV, PTS) |> 
  mutate_all(scale) |> 
  na.omit()
# function to compute total within-cluster sum of square 
wss <- function(k) {
  kmeans(playersscaled, k, nstart = 10 )$tot.withinss
}

# Compute and plot wss for k = 1 to k = 15
k.values <- 1:15

# extract wss for 2-15 clusters
wss_values <- map_dbl(k.values, wss)

plot(k.values, wss_values,
       type="b", pch = 19, frame = FALSE, 
       xlab="Number of clusters K",
       ylab="Total within-clusters sum of squares")
clusters <- kmeans(playersscaled, centers = 5, nstart = 25)
clusters
playercluster <- data.frame(playersselected, clusters$cluster) 
kt <- playercluster |> filter(Player == "Keisei Tominaga")

kt
ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=playercluster, aes(x=MP, y=PTS, color=clusters.cluster)) + 
  geom_point(data=kt, aes(x=MP, y=PTS), color="red")
big10 <- c("Nebraska Cornhuskers Men's", "Iowa Hawkeyes Men's", "Minnesota Golden Gophers Men's", "Illinois Fighting Illini Men's", "Northwestern Wildcats Men's", "Wisconsin Badgers Men's", "Indiana Hoosiers Men's", "Purdue Boilermakers Men's", "Ohio State Buckeyes Men's", "Michigan Wolverines Men's", "Michigan State Spartans Men's", "Penn State Nittany Lions Men's", "Rutgers Scarlet Knights Men's", "Maryland Terrapins Men's")

playercluster |> filter(clusters.cluster == kt$clusters.cluster) |> filter(Team %in% big10) |> arrange(desc(MP))

description: Learn how to group like things together with math.
text: - K-means Clustering: A machine learning algorithm that groups similar data points into a specified number of clusters based on their features. - Euclidean Distance: The straight-line distance between two points in a multi-dimensional space, used in k-means clustering to measure similarity between data points. - Cluster: A group of data points in a k-means analysis that are more similar to each other than to data points in other clusters. - Centroid: The mean point of all data points within a cluster, representing the cluster's center. - Elbow Method: A technique used to determine the optimal number of clusters (k) by plotting the sum of squared distances against different k values and looking for an "elbow" in the curve. - Scaling: The process of standardizing variables to a common scale to ensure each feature contributes equally to the clustering analysis.

description: Learn how to determine if it was bad luck or some kind of cosmic weirdness.
text: The goal of this lesson is to introduce you to the concept of statistical simulation and its applications in sports analytics. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand how to use simulations to determine whether unusual performances or streaks are likely due to random chance or indicative of a significant change in performance. We'll focus on using R's random binomial distribution function to simulate shooting percentages over multiple trials. You'll learn how to interpret the results of these simulations, understand the concept of statistical distributions, and apply this knowledge to real-world sports scenarios. This skill will enhance your ability to differentiate between genuine performance issues and normal variability in sports data, providing valuable insights for player evaluation, strategy development, and performance analysis.

description: Learn how to determine if it was bad luck or some kind of cosmic weirdness.
text: In the 2017-2018 season, James Palmer Jr. took 139 three point attempts and made 43 of them for a .309 shooting percentage. A few weeks into the next season, he was 7 for 39 -- a paltry .179. Is something wrong or is this just bad luck? Luck is something that comes up a lot in sports. Is a team unlucky? Or a player? One way we can get to this, we can get to that is by simulating things based on their typical percentages. Simulations work by choosing random values within a range based on a distribution. The most common distribution is the normal or binomial distribution. The normal distribution is where the most cases appear around the mean, 66 percent of cases are within one standard deviation from the mean, and the further away from the mean you get, the more rare things become. Let's simulate 39 three point attempts 1000 times with his season long shooting percentage and see if this could just be random chance or something else. We do this using a base R function called `rbinom` or binomial distribution. So what that means is there's a normally distrubuted chance that James Palmer Jr. is going to shoot above and below his career three point shooting percentage. If we randomly assign values in that distribution 1000 times, how many times will it come up 7, like this example? How do we read this? The first row and the second row form a pair. The top row is the number of shots made. The number immediately under it is the number of simulations where that occurred. So what we see is given his season long shooting percentage, it's not out of the realm of randomness that with just 39 attempts for Palmer, he's only hit only 7. In 1000 simulations, it comes up 35 times. Is he below where he should be? Yes. Will he likely improve and soon? Unless something is very wrong, yes. And indeed, by the end of the season, he finished with a .313 shooting percentage from 3 point range. So we can say he was just unlucky.
code:
set.seed(1234)

simulations <- rbinom(n = 1000, size = 39, prob = .309)

table(simulations)

description: Learn how to determine if it was bad luck or some kind of cosmic weirdness.
text: During the Western Illinois game in the 2018-2019 season, the team, shooting .329 on the season from behind the arc, went 0-15 in the second half. How strange is that? Short answer: Really weird. If you simulate 15 threes 1000 times, sometimes you'll see them miss all of them, but only a few times -- five times, in this case. Most of the time, the team won't go 0-15 even once. So going ice cold is not totally out of the realm of random chance, but it's highly unlikely.
code:
set.seed(1234)

simulations <- rbinom(n = 1000, size = 15, prob = .329)

table(simulations)

description: Learn how to determine if it was bad luck or some kind of cosmic weirdness.
text: In this lesson, we've explored the powerful technique of statistical simulation and its application in sports analytics. We began by understanding the concept of normal distribution and how it relates to player performance. Using R's rbinom() function, we learned how to simulate shooting percentages over multiple trials, applying this to real examples like James Palmer Jr.'s three-point shooting slump. We discovered how to interpret the results of these simulations, determining whether observed performances are within the realm of normal variability or truly unusual. We also applied this technique to team performance, analyzing the likelihood of extreme cold streaks. This hands-on approach has equipped you with the skills to use simulations for evaluating player and team performances, helping to distinguish between random fluctuations and genuine issues. Remember, while simulations provide valuable insights, they should be used in conjunction with other analytical tools and contextual knowledge of the sport for comprehensive analysis.

description: Learn how to determine if it was bad luck or some kind of cosmic weirdness.
text: - Simulation: A statistical technique that uses random sampling to model real-world scenarios and estimate probabilities of different outcomes. - Normal Distribution: A symmetrical, bell-shaped probability distribution where data tends to cluster around the mean, with decreasing frequency as values move away from the center. - Binomial Distribution: A probability distribution that represents the number of successes in a fixed number of independent trials, each with the same probability of success. - `rbinom()`: An R function used to generate random numbers from a binomial distribution, often used in simulations. - Set Seed: A function used to initialize the random number generator, ensuring reproducibility in simulations. - Probability: The likelihood of an event occurring, expressed as a number between 0 and 1. - Standard Deviation: A measure of the amount of variation or dispersion of a set of values from their mean. - Random Variability: Natural fluctuations in performance or outcomes that occur by chance rather than due to a specific cause.

description: Learn how to combine two datasets together based on a common field.
text: The goal of this lesson is to introduce you to the concept of data joining, a crucial skill in sports analytics for combining information from multiple sources. By the end of this tutorial, you'll understand how to use various join functions in R to merge datasets based on common identifiers. We'll explore different types of joins including inner, left, right, and full joins, and learn when to apply each. You'll practice combining player statistics with team information, merging data across seasons, and handling common challenges in data joining such as mismatched keys and duplicate entries. This skill will enhance your ability to create more comprehensive datasets for analysis, allowing you to uncover insights that may not be apparent in isolated datasets. Whether you're comparing player performance across leagues, integrating scouting reports with game statistics, or building historical trend analyses, mastering data joining techniques will significantly expand your capabilities in sports data analysis.

description: Learn how to combine two datasets together based on a common field.
text: Sometimes we have information in two different tables that we need to be in on. For this, we're going to take the stats from a game and join it to the schedule, so the home and away teams have their stats attached to it. How are we going to do that? By using joins -- a powerful tool that solves this problem. First we're going to load the data. Now, we need data. In the `hoopR` library, we have access to team box scores and the schedule. This is great if we want to try and predict who is going to win a basketball game based on their stats. We can calculate season metrics up to that point in the schedule and then look at two teams going into the game. The problem with the team box scores is that it's just one team. We don't get the home and away teams. The problem with the schedule is we don't know the stats of the teams -- we just know who is the home and the away team. In other words, we have data in two different tables and we need it together. Enter joins. Let's first get set up with data. This next block will pull the most recent season of men's college basketball data and the schedule. It will also do some quick cleaning of the team box scores to turn numbers into numbers and split the makes vs attempts columns into separate makes and attempts columns.
code:
teamgames <- load_mbb_team_box(seasons = most_recent_mbb_season()) |>
  separate(field_goals_made_field_goals_attempted, into = c("field_goals_made","field_goals_attempted")) |>
  separate(three_point_field_goals_made_three_point_field_goals_attempted, into = c("three_point_field_goals_made","three_point_field_goals_attempted")) |>
  separate(free_throws_made_free_throws_attempted, into = c("free_throws_made","free_throws_attempted")) |>
  mutate_at(12:35, as.numeric)

schedule <- load_mbb_schedule(seasons = most_recent_mbb_season())

description: Learn how to combine two datasets together based on a common field.
text: The first thing that we need when joining data together is we need columns in both tables that match each other. Generally speaking, we're looking for ID numbers that uniquely identify things like teams and games. That means we need to first look at our data and see what we have. Let's first look at the schedule. If you explore around in that data, you'll see two different kinds of IDs. There's columns that have XXXX.id after them, and some have XXXX.uid after them. The difference is not really that important -- it's unique to the data provider -- but both represent ID numbers for that thing. For example `home.uid` and `away.uid` represent the id numbers of the home and away team. The `id` column is the ID number for this particular game. If we use both, we can get the home team for that specific game -- meaning the home team at that moment. That's important. Now let's look at the teamgames data, to see if we can find similar id numbers. This sometimes take a little bit of deduction, but if you look, you'll see `game_id`, which appears to match the `id` from the schedule dataset. Because we only have one team here, we won't have `home.uid` or `away.uid` ... but we do have `team_uid`, which appears to match the form of the schedule uids. That means we're in business.
code:
head(schedule)
head(teamgames)

description: Learn how to combine two datasets together based on a common field.
text: Sometimes, you can jump straight to joining data -- your datasets are lean, have no extraneous information and will seamlessly come together without issue. The other 99 percent of the time, your first steps are to thin your data up to just what you need. In our case, we're going to do a couple of things. First, we're going to calculate the total number of points the team scored. It's not in the data, but we can calculate it by multiplying three pointers by 3, subtracting threes made from all field goals made and mulitplying that by 2, and then adding in free throws. Then, for future work, we're going to calculate the rolling mean of shooting percentage lagged by a game. In other words, what were the 5 game rolling mean of shooting percentage going into this game. Lastly, we'll use select to get us the six columns we need. We'll put all of this into a new dataframe called teamstats. Now we're going to do the same thing with the schedule. However, we have a problem here -- a game has two teams. A home team and an away team. We have no way to know, from our teamstats data, who was the home or away team. To make this work, we need to make two dataframes -- one for home teams and one for away teams. And now, we've arrived at the point where we can join the data. The problem we now face is what *kind* of join do we want to do? There's multiple kinds. The three we'll talk about here are the `left_join`, the `right_join` and the `inner_join`. The best way to think about this is to think of your data as two sheets of paper -- the schedule sheet is on the left, the teamstats sheet is on the right. A `left_join` would take all data from the schedule sheet and keep *only* the data that matched from the right sheet. The `right_join` is the opposite -- all teamstats data is kept, and only the matching schedule data is kept. The `inner_join` keeps only the data where both match. Since we're going to be making models out of this data, we only want the data where both matches, so `inner_join` it is. The next issue is the column names are not the same. Joining in dplyr is easiest when the id column names are the same. Alas, we aren't so lucky. So we have to specify which field equals which. And the order matters. One last bit of housekeeping -- when the data comes together, we'll need to rename some columns so they are home and away team specific. Here's what that looks like: Behind the scenes, I've gone ahead and made a home and away dataframe that is the result of the above code, as well as one that does the away side too. The last thing we need to do is put it all together. When we have clean data, with matching field names, joins are pretty easy.
code:
teamstats <- teamgames |> 
  group_by(team_short_display_name) |>
  mutate(
    team_score = ((field_goals_made-three_point_field_goals_made) * 2) + (three_point_field_goals_made*3) + free_throws_made,
    rolling_shooting_percentage = rollmean(lag(field_goal_pct, n=1), k=5, fill=field_goal_pct)
    ) |>
  select(team_short_display_name, team_uid, game_id, game_date, team_score, rolling_shooting_percentage)
home_games <- schedule |>
  select(id, home.uid)

away_games <- schedule |>
  select(id, away.uid)
home_games |> 
  inner_join(teamstats, by=c("id"="game_id", "home.uid"="team_uid")) |>
  rename(
    home_team_short_display_name = team_short_display_name, 
    home_team_score=team_score, 
    home_rolling_shooting_percentage = rolling_shooting_percentage
    )
home |> inner_join(away)

description: Learn how to combine two datasets together based on a common field.
text: In this lesson, we've explored the essential skill of data joining and its application in sports analytics. We began by understanding the concept of key variables and how they serve as the bridge between datasets. We practiced using various join functions in R, including inner_join(), left_join(), right_join(), and full_join(), learning the unique characteristics and use cases for each. Through hands-on examples, we combined player statistics with team information, merged data across multiple seasons, and tackled common challenges such as handling mismatched keys and dealing with duplicate entries. We also explored the importance of data cleaning and standardization before joining, ensuring accurate and meaningful results. By working with real sports data, you've gained practical experience in creating more comprehensive datasets that allow for deeper analysis and insights. Remember, effective data joining is not just about combining tables, but about thoughtfully considering which data to include and how it will impact your analysis. This skill will prove invaluable as you continue to work with complex, multi-source datasets in your sports analytics projects, enabling you to uncover relationships and trends that might otherwise remain hidden in separate data silos.

description: Learn how to combine two datasets together based on a common field.
text: - Join: A method of combining data from two or more tables based on a related column between them. - Key: A column or set of columns used to establish a relationship between tables in a join operation. - Inner Join: A type of join that returns only the rows where there is a match in both tables being joined. - Left Join: A join that returns all rows from the left table and the matched rows from the right table. If there is no match, NULL values are returned for columns from the right table. - Right Join: Similar to a left join, but returns all rows from the right table and the matched rows from the left table. - Full Join: A join that returns all rows when there is a match in either the left or right table. If there is no match, NULL values are returned for columns from the table without a match.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 38: Getting CSVs from Sports Reference
description: Learn how to export any Sports Reference table as a CSV and import it into R.
text: The goal of this lesson is to teach you how to get data from Sports Reference sites (Sports-Reference.com, Basketball-Reference.com, Baseball-Reference.com, Pro-Football-Reference.com, etc.) into R. Every table on Sports Reference can be turned into a CSV using a copy-and-paste workflow. By the end of this tutorial, you'll know the step-by-step process for exporting table data, cleaning it up in a text file, saving it as a CSV, and importing it into R for analysis.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 38: Getting CSVs from Sports Reference
description: Learn how to export any Sports Reference table as a CSV and import it into R.
text: Sports Reference is one of the richest free sources of historical sports statistics on the internet. It covers the NFL, NBA, MLB, NHL, college football, college basketball, and more, going back decades. The catch: the data lives on a website, not in a pre-packaged R dataset. Fortunately, Sports Reference makes it easy to extract any table as comma-separated text. Once you know the workflow, you can turn virtually any table on the site into a CSV file in minutes.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 38: Getting CSVs from Sports Reference
description: Learn how to export any Sports Reference table as a CSV and import it into R.
section: Step 1: Open a Plain Text File in RStudio
text: Before you do anything on the Sports Reference website, open a plain text file in RStudio. You'll use this as a temporary clipboard for the raw CSV text. In RStudio, go to **File > New File > Text File**. This opens a blank file with no formatting — exactly what you want. A Word document or Google Doc will add invisible characters that corrupt your data.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 38: Getting CSVs from Sports Reference
description: Learn how to export any Sports Reference table as a CSV and import it into R.
section: Step 2: Find Your Table and Open the Export Menu
text: Navigate to the Sports Reference page that has the table you want. Every data table on Sports Reference has a **Share & Export** button located just above the table (sometimes you need to hover over the table for it to appear). Click **Share & Export**, then choose **Get table as CSV (for Excel)**. This switches the table display from formatted HTML to raw comma-separated text. **Important note:** Many Sports Reference tables have **two header rows** — one with main column categories and one with sub-categories. You will need to deal with this. More on that in Step 4.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 38: Getting CSVs from Sports Reference
description: Learn how to export any Sports Reference table as a CSV and import it into R.
section: Step 3: Highlight and Copy the Data
text: With the table now displayed as CSV text, highlight all of it — from the very first character of the header row down to the last row of data — and copy it (`Cmd+C` on Mac, `Ctrl+C` on Windows). Make sure you get everything. Missing even one row is easy to do if you're not careful about selecting to the very bottom.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 38: Getting CSVs from Sports Reference
description: Learn how to export any Sports Reference table as a CSV and import it into R.
section: Step 4: Paste Into Your Text File and Fix the Headers
text: Switch to your plain text file in RStudio and paste the data (`Cmd+V` / `Ctrl+V`). Now look at your headers carefully. R — and virtually every other data analysis tool — requires **exactly one header row**. If Sports Reference gave you two header rows, you need to fix that now. Look at the first two rows of your pasted data. The first row will have the main column names. The second row will have sub-category names. Your job is to: 1. Decide which header row to keep (usually a combination: keep row 2 but replace any blank or repeated names with the descriptive name from row 1). 2. Delete the first header row so that row 1 is headers and row 2 is your first data row. Also look for **duplicate column names** within a single row. For example, if a table has both total goals and goals-per-game, both might be labeled `G`. You must rename one of them (e.g., `G` and `G_per_game`) so every column name is unique. R will let you import duplicate column names, but it will auto-rename them in confusing ways, so it's better to fix them now.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 38: Getting CSVs from Sports Reference
description: Learn how to export any Sports Reference table as a CSV and import it into R.
section: Step 5: Delete Any Repeated Header Rows Inside the Data
text: Sports Reference sometimes inserts a repeated header row every 20–25 rows within the data itself (it's a web display feature). These look just like the header row but appear in the middle of your data. Scan through your text file and delete any rows that repeat your column names. They're easy to spot — they'll have the same text as your header row appearing in the middle of the data.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 38: Getting CSVs from Sports Reference
description: Learn how to export any Sports Reference table as a CSV and import it into R.
text: Once your headers are clean and any duplicate header rows are removed, save the file. In RStudio, go to **File > Save As**. Give your file a meaningful name — something that describes what's in it, like `nebraska_football_2024.csv` — and make sure the filename ends in `.csv`. Save it in the same folder as your R project or in a `data` subfolder within your project. **Do not save it as a `.txt` file.** The `.csv` extension tells R and other tools that the file uses commas to separate values.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 38: Getting CSVs from Sports Reference
description: Learn how to export any Sports Reference table as a CSV and import it into R.
text: Now import your data just like any other CSV file: ```r library(tidyverse) your_data <- read_csv("nebraska_football_2024.csv") ``` Replace `your_data` with a descriptive variable name and `nebraska_football_2024.csv` with your actual filename (including the path if it's in a subfolder, e.g., `"data/nebraska_football_2024.csv"`). If you get errors on import, the most common causes are: - **Leftover duplicate header rows** in the middle of the data - **Non-standard characters** in team or player names (common with international athletes) - **Mismatched column counts** because a row has an extra or missing comma Use `head()` and `glimpse()` to inspect your imported data and confirm it looks correct before moving on to analysis.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 38: Getting CSVs from Sports Reference
description: Learn how to export any Sports Reference table as a CSV and import it into R.
text: In this lesson, you learned a repeatable workflow for getting data out of Sports Reference and into R: 1. Open a plain text file in RStudio first. 2. Use Sports Reference's **Share & Export > Get table as CSV** option. 3. Highlight and copy all the data. 4. Paste into your text file. 5. Fix headers: keep only one header row, rename any duplicate column names. 6. Delete any repeated header rows that appear inside the data. 7. Save the file with a `.csv` extension. 8. Import with `read_csv()`. This workflow works for any table on any Sports Reference site. Once you've done it a few times, it takes under two minutes to go from website table to imported R data frame.

tutorial: Sports Data Lesson 38: Getting CSVs from Sports Reference
description: Learn how to export any Sports Reference table as a CSV and import it into R.
text: - **Sports Reference**: A family of websites (Sports-Reference.com, Basketball-Reference.com, Baseball-Reference.com, Pro-Football-Reference.com) providing comprehensive historical sports statistics. - **CSV (Comma-Separated Values)**: A plain text file format where each value is separated by a comma and each row is on its own line, widely used for storing and transferring tabular data. - **Share & Export**: The Sports Reference menu option on each data table that allows you to export the table as CSV text. - **Header row**: The first row of a data file that contains the column names rather than data values. R requires exactly one header row. - **Duplicate column names**: When two columns in the same dataset share the same name, which R will handle automatically but confusingly — best renamed manually before import. - **Plain text file**: A file containing only unformatted characters, with no hidden markup or formatting codes — the correct file type to use as a staging area for CSV data. - **`read_csv()`**: The tidyverse function used to import a CSV file into R as a data frame.

description: A checklist for your blog post projects in SPMC 350.
text: The goal of this lesson is to give you a reference checklist for your blog post projects. The main projects in SPMC 350 Sports Data Analysis and Visualization involve writing a blog post, published on GitHub Pages, about a sports topic that uses code, data, and three graphics to tell a story. The assignment is worth 20 percent of the semester grade. An example of a B+/A- post: [Is Nebraska the best worst team in college basketball?](http://mattwaite.github.io/posts/is-nebraska-the-best-worst-basketball-team/) Use this checklist before you submit. Go through every item. If you can't check something off, fix it first.

description: A checklist for your blog post projects in SPMC 350.
text: Before you spend hours on analysis, make sure your topic is worth pursuing. 1. Is your topic **original**? Is it **interesting**? 2. Does your topic ask if Tom Brady, Michael Jordan, LeBron James, the 95 Huskers, Alabama football, or Georgia's 2021 defense is the GOAT? Then your topic is **not** original nor interesting. 3. Seriously. Anything that asks if Alabama is good, or the Golden State Warriors, or Nicola Jokic, or any team that's won more than one championship in a relatively short period of time — if you're asking if they're good, stop. Find something else. 4. There's a very, very, very big world out there. Read. Listen. Find something new. 5. Still stuck? Sometimes good projects come from pain. A decent idea might be: is X as bad as we think? One exception: if your idea is "Nebraska is the best 3-9 team of all time," stop. It's played out. Find another cosmically snake-bitten team or player. 6. Another idea: What sports are in season? What's happening — good or bad? Zero in on that.

description: A checklist for your blog post projects in SPMC 350.
text: Good analysis buried in bad writing doesn't get read. 1. Have you **spell checked** your writing? 2. Have you **read what you have written out loud**? Reading it out loud will help you find bad writing. 3. No really. Read it out loud. It makes a huge difference. If you stumble over your own words, you should rewrite it. 4. If what you read out loud doesn't sound like **you**, rewrite it. 5. Do you have **complete sentences**? Do you have **active verbs**?

description: A checklist for your blog post projects in SPMC 350.
text: Headlines are not labels. Headlines tell stories. 1. Are your headlines just labels? Examples: *Nebraska's offense. NBA's Best Shooters. The best NFL punters.* Those are labels, not headlines. 2. Do your headlines **tell a story**, or attempt to draw the reader into one? 3. Do your headlines include words that describe **what the chart is about**? 4. Do your headlines **point me toward what I should see** in the chart?

description: A checklist for your blog post projects in SPMC 350.
text: Every chart needs these things. No exceptions. 1. Do you have the **required parts**? They are: headline, chatter, credit line, source line. 2. Do your graphics have **annotations** that help me determine what is going on? Example: Are important dots labeled on a scatterplot? 3. Are there **lines to show averages**? Are those lines labeled? 4. Can I **read bar chart axis text**? 5. Does each chart **tell a story**? Can I tell what that story is in a glance? 6. Do you use **color sparingly** to draw attention to something specific? 7. Is there **separation between your headline size and your chatter size**? 8. Does your typography have a **hierarchy**? Does your headline stand out from your chatter? Are your axis labels smaller than your data labels? 9. Have you **simplified the theme** (i.e., dropped the default grey background)? 10. Are your **axis labels** something anyone can understand? 11. Does your `theme()` element include `plot.title.position = "plot"` to move the headline flush left instead of that weird indent?

description: A checklist for your blog post projects in SPMC 350.
text: Clean code is part of the grade. 1. Are your **variables named** what they are? If you called your data `logs` when it's made up of players, that's bad. 2. Is your code **spaced out** so it's one line per command? Look at the example post for guidance. 3. Is your code in the **proper order**? Libraries at the top. Data preparation before the graphics. 4. **Did you add `message=FALSE, warning=FALSE`** to any `{r}` blocks that spit out large amounts of automated text? Your library step doesn't need all that gibberish spitting out in your blog.

description: A checklist for your blog post projects in SPMC 350.
text: The link you are turning in starts with `YourGitHubUserNameHere.github.io` **but is not just your `username.github.io` URL**. **Submit the link to the post**. If you are turning in a `GitHub.com` URL, you are turning in the wrong thing.

description: A checklist for your blog post projects in SPMC 350.
text: - **Quarto blog**: The publishing format used for your blog posts — a combination of Markdown prose and R code chunks that renders to a static website. - **GitHub Pages**: The free hosting service that publishes your Quarto blog as a live website at `username.github.io`. - **Headline**: The main title of a visualization that captures the key message or story of the graphic — not a label. - **Chatter**: The subtitle or explanatory text below the headline that provides context about the visualization. - **Credit line**: Text in the graphic identifying who made it (you). - **Source line**: Text in the graphic identifying where the data came from. - **Annotation**: Text or graphical elements added to a chart to highlight or explain specific data points. - **`plot.title.position = "plot"`**: A `theme()` argument that aligns the plot title flush with the left edge of the plot area rather than the default indented position. - **`message=FALSE, warning=FALSE`**: Arguments added to an R code chunk header to suppress startup messages and warnings from appearing in the rendered blog post.
